source.title: Batching defaults rollout checklist (runtime-1.22) — WIP
source.body:
Owner: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
Primary eng owner: Sofia Ivanova (Scheduling)
Runtime train: 1.22
Feature: Workload-aware batching defaults w/ 3 SLO tiers (fast / standard / cost-efficient)
Last reviewed in: #eng-sre, #eng-runtime, #eng-releases (see linked artifacts)

Purpose
- Working checklist for staged rollout of tiered batching defaults.
- Includes: prerequisites, canary steps, dashboards to watch, abort criteria, comms templates, and rollback pointers.
- This doc intentionally mixes “done / TODO / notes”. Keep it current during the rollout window.

Definitions (quick)
- Tiered defaults: server chooses a batching profile mapped to a customer-facing tier (fast/standard/cost-efficient) plus workload signals (prompt length buckets, streaming flag, concurrency/queue pressure).
- Override hierarchy (expected): customer override > route policy > tier default > model/hardware safety constraints > overload mode fallback.
- Rollout units:
  - Hosted: by region + percentage of traffic for eligible routes/models.
  - Dedicated: opt-in per tenant/cluster; allow per-tenant overrides.
  - Private: no default flip in this rollout (requires customer-managed upgrade / separate enablement).

Pre-flight checklist (must be true before any canary flip)
1) Code + config readiness
- [ ] Runtime 1.22 build cut includes: feature flag + kill switch, tier schema, reason codes, tier-labeled metrics w/ cardinality controls.
- [ ] Control plane config supports:
  - route/model policy tier selection
  - explicit “disabled / legacy behavior” mode
  - per-tenant (Dedicated) override precedence.
- [ ] Reason codes emitted + documented (at least):
  - tier_default_applied
  - route_policy_override
  - dedicated_tenant_override
  - model_constraint_fallback
  - overload_mode_enabled
  - streaming_protection_applied
  - guardrail_clamp (batch size/wait budget clamped)

2) Observability readiness (dashboards, alerts, sampling)
- [ ] Dashboards deployed to prod (observability-pack/pr-933) and validated in at least 2 regions.
- [ ] Alerts created + routed (paging vs ticketing finalized) for:
  - fast tier p99 regression
  - queue depth blowups / sustained saturation
  - telemetry cardinality / ingestion cost spikes
  - error rate/regression signals (timeouts, 429/503, cancellations)
- [ ] Metrics cardinality controls confirmed (hotfix backport if needed; see pr-28544 if applicable).

3) Benchmark + gate readiness
- [ ] Perf suite mixed workload scenarios landed (benchmark-lab/pr-612).
- [ ] Perf canary gating on tiered SLOs enabled and baselines refreshed/approved (perf-canary/pr-489).
- [ ] Acceptance thresholds agreed (see SLO tier doc); note: use region-specific baselines where needed.

4) Support + comms readiness
- [ ] Support playbook updated (common questions: “what happens if I don’t pick a tier?”, “how to force fast tier for streaming?”, “why is my override not taking effect?”).
- [ ] Customer-facing docs merged (redwood-docs/pr-1882) and release notes drafted (runtime 1.22 notes).
- [ ] Status page playbook prepared if latency worsens during canary.

Gating requirements (minimum green signals)
- No open P0 incidents in the last 72h affecting inference latency/availability.
- Error budget burn (hosted inference) stable (no sustained burn > 1x baseline for 24h).
- Fleet capacity headroom confirmed for canary regions (especially fast tier).

Dashboards to watch (pin these before starting)
Core
- “Tiered batching: latency by tier/model/region”
  - p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency
  - streaming: time-to-first-token (TTFT) + inter-token latency (ITL)
- “Scheduler health”
  - queue depth, queue wait time, admission rejects
  - continuous batching: batch size distribution, max_wait_ms utilization
  - prefill vs decode utilization split
- “Overload mode + backpressure”
  - load shedding counters (429/503), overload_mode_enabled rate
  - cancellations/timeouts
Economics
- “Cost + throughput”
  - tokens/sec/GPU, requests/sec
  - $/1K tokens estimate (metering proxy)
  - cache hit rate (prefix/KV) where applicable
Quality / correctness proxies
- “Output health signals” (as available)
  - truncation rate / max_tokens clamps
  - tool/function call schema failures (if relevant)
  - error codes by reason
Telemetry safety
- “Metrics ingestion + cardinality”
  - unique series count (tier label + model + region)
  - ingestion lag, dropped points, cost anomaly alerts

Abort criteria (stop expansion and/or rollback)
Severity levels
- SEV-0: immediate kill switch (within minutes)
- SEV-1: pause rollout + revert canary to previous state (same day)
- SEV-2: hold at current % and investigate (24–48h)

SEV-0 triggers (kill switch now)
- Fast tier p99 latency regression >= +25% vs baseline for 30 minutes in any canary region for top-3 models OR any single enterprise priority route.
- Any sustained availability impact attributable to the change:
  - 5xx rate >= 2x baseline for 15 minutes (hosted inference)
  - timeouts/cancellations >= 2x baseline for 15 minutes
- Scheduler instability:
  - runaway queue depth (monotonic growth) for > 10 minutes with no recovery AND admission control not stabilizing.
- Telemetry incident:
  - metric series cardinality > agreed cap (see Observability spec) for > 10 minutes OR ingestion costs spike to paging threshold.

SEV-1 triggers (pause + revert canary)
- Standard tier p95 regression >= +15% for 60 minutes.
- TTFT regression for streaming routes >= +20% for 30 minutes.
- Increased 429/503 due to backpressure without corresponding improvement in tail latency.
- Dedicated: tenant reports override precedence wrong + reproducible (customer-impacting) (reference SUP-48271 class issues).

SEV-2 triggers (hold, investigate)
- Throughput/cost improvements not materializing (<= +2% tokens/sec) after 24h at steady traffic.
- Unexpected profile selection distribution (e.g., overload_mode_enabled > 1% for long periods; model_constraint_fallback frequent).
- Support ticket spike about “why did latency change?” even if metrics show small deltas (messaging/expectations risk).

Rollout steps (staged)
Step 0 — Dry run (staging)
- [ ] Enable tiered defaults in staging at 100% for at least 2 hours with synthetic load (mixed prompt lengths + streaming/non-streaming).
- [ ] Validate reason codes show expected distribution.
- [ ] Validate dashboards show tier breakdown.
- [ ] Validate kill switch and per-route disable path works.
Notes: staging is necessary but insufficient; primary risks show up only under real concurrency distributions.

Step 1 — Internal canary (prod, Redwood-owned traffic)
Scope
- Enable for Redwood internal routes only (dogfood) in 1 region.
- Target tier coverage: fast + standard only initially (leave cost-efficient off unless explicitly needed).
Checklist
- [ ] Feature flag on for internal canary cohort (confirm config commit hash).
- [ ] Confirm which models are eligible (top model families used by internal apps).
- [ ] Monitor for 2–4 hours:
  - p99/p95, TTFT, queue depth, overload counters
  - reason codes: ensure tier_default_applied dominates; check for unexpected model_constraint_fallback
Exit criteria
- [ ] No SEV-1/0 triggers.
- [ ] At least stable performance; minor regressions investigated/understood.

Step 2 — Hosted API subset (external, small %)
Scope
- Start at 1% of eligible traffic in 1 region (prefer region w/ good headroom).
- Eligible endpoints: chat + generate; exclude any known “fragile” routes (large context, unusual streaming patterns) until later.
Ramp plan (default)
- 1% → 5% → 10% → 25% → 50% → 100% (per region), one step per 24h if green.
Checklist per ramp
- [ ] Pre-ramp snapshot: baselines (24h) for latency/error/cost.
- [ ] Turn on ramp; annotate dashboards with change marker.
- [ ] Monitor first 30 minutes continuously; then hourly checks.
- [ ] Verify perf canary and alerts remain green.
- [ ] Review reason codes: ensure no silent override precedence issues.
Exit criteria per ramp
- [ ] Meets tier SLO targets per published definition.
- [ ] Error budget burn not elevated.
- [ ] Support tickets stable.

Step 3 — Dedicated opt-in (per tenant/cluster)
Scope
- Opt-in only, coordinated with Dedicated PM (Lauren Bishop) + CS.
- Start with 1–2 friendly tenants with mixed workloads.
Checklist
- [ ] Confirm per-tenant override precedence and UI/CLI path for settings.
- [ ] Validate “effective profile” introspection (Console shows what’s applied + why).
- [ ] Run tenant-specific observation window (min 48h) due to traffic variability.
- [ ] Confirm rollback path for tenant is immediate (tenant-level disable) without affecting other tenants.
Exit criteria
- [ ] No tenant-specific p95/p99 regressions beyond thresholds.
- [ ] Override precedence support questions answered; docs link provided.

Step 4 — Broader default enablement (Hosted)
Scope
- Expand to additional regions.
- Consider enabling cost-efficient tier defaults for eligible workloads after fast/standard stable.
Checklist
- [ ] Ensure capacity planning OK (cost-efficient may increase batch size; watch queue blowups).
- [ ] Re-validate telemetry cardinality across multi-region.
- [ ] Confirm docs and SDK tier parameter released (python/typescript) if required for customers.

Operational runbook: kill switch + rollback
Immediate kill switch (global)
- Owner on call: SRE (Rafael) or Runtime on call (Scheduling).
- Action: flip global flag “tiered_batching_defaults_enabled=false” (control plane).
- Expected effect: revert to legacy/manual batching behavior (or previous default behavior) within propagation window.
- Follow-up: postmortem-style notes in incident channel if SEV-0.

Scoped rollback (recommended first when possible)
- Disable for:
  - specific region
  - specific model family
  - specific route
  - specific Dedicated tenant
- Use when impact is localized or model-specific.

Rollback validation checklist
- [ ] Confirm reason codes switch from tier_default_applied to legacy/disabled (or explicit “disabled” code).
- [ ] Confirm latency returns toward baseline (allow 10–20 min for queues to drain).
- [ ] Confirm queue depth normalizes.
- [ ] Confirm error rates normalize.

Comms templates (copy/paste)
1) Internal (Slack #eng-runtime / #eng-sre)
Subject: [Rollout] tiered batching defaults — ramp to <X%> in <region>
Body:
- Change: enable tiered batching defaults (runtime 1.22) at <X%> for eligible hosted traffic in <region>
- Start time: <UTC timestamp>
- Owners: SRE <name>, Runtime <name>
- Dashboards: <links>
- Abort criteria: SEV-0/1 per rollout checklist; kill switch ready
- Notes: watch fast p99 + streaming TTFT; watch metric cardinality

2) Customer support macro (if customers ask “why did latency/cost change?”)
Thanks for reaching out — we’re rolling out updated batching defaults that map to our latency SLO tiers (fast/standard/cost-efficient). This change only affects runtime scheduling; it does not change data handling/logging. If you share:
- route name / model
- streaming vs non-streaming
- region
- timeframe + request IDs
we can confirm the effective tier/profile and suggest either (a) a tier selection, or (b) an override if needed.

3) Status page (only if customer-facing impact)
Investigating elevated latency for a subset of inference requests in <region>. We’ve paused a recent runtime configuration change and are rolling back to prior settings. Next update in <30 min>.

4) Direct email to Dedicated opt-in tenant (pre-change)
We’re enabling tiered batching defaults (fast/standard/cost-efficient) for your Dedicated cluster as an opt-in performance/cost feature. We will start with <tier> and monitor p95/p99 latency and throughput. You can override or disable at any time; we’ll share a Console screenshot and the exact config key. No changes to logging/retention or data access.

Known sharp edges / “gotchas” to watch
- Metric cardinality blow-up if tier/model/route labels combine without sampling/limits.
- Mixed streaming + long prompts: starvation risk if wait budgets too tight; watch fast tier carefully.
- Overload behavior: make sure we’re not “solving” overload by just increasing batching (tail latency can worsen).
- Dedicated overrides: precedence confusion is common; ensure Console shows effective config and reason code.

Open TODOs (as of last_modified)
- [ ] Add explicit link to overload behavior runbook (ENG-18433) once published.
- [ ] Decide paging thresholds: fast-tier p99 regression (debate: page vs ticket) — see #eng-sre 1768523309.
- [ ] Finalize ramp schedule per region (coordinate w/ fleet readiness).
- [ ] Add “baseline refresh” process snippet for perf canary (INT-33055).

Change log notes (quick)
- 2026-03-08: initial checklist drafted from SRE + runtime threads.
- 2026-03-18: added telemetry/cardinality abort criteria after early canary spike discussions.
- 2026-04-24: updated staged ramp steps + comms templates; aligned with docs readiness email.

- “Overload mode + backpressure”
  - load shedding counters (429/503), overload_mode_enabled rate
  - cancellations/timeouts
Economics
- “Cost + throughput”
  - tokens/sec/GPU, requests/sec
  - $/1K tokens estimate (metering proxy)
  - cache hit rate (prefix/KV) where applicable
Quality / correctness proxies
- “Output health signals” (as available)
  - truncation rate / max_tokens clamps
  - tool/function call schema failures (if relevant)
  - error codes by reason
Telemetry safety
- “Metrics ingestion + cardinality”
  - unique series count (tier label + model + region)
  - ingestion lag, dropped points, cost anomaly alerts

Abort criteria (stop expansion and/or rollback)
Severity levels
- SEV-0: immediate kill switch (within minutes)
- SEV-1: pause rollout + revert canary to previous state (same day)
- SEV-2: hold at current % and investigate (24–48h)

SEV-0 triggers (kill switch now)
- Fast tier p99 latency regression >= +25% vs baseline for 30 minutes in any canary region for top-3 models OR any single enterprise priority route.
- Any sustained availability impact attributable to the change:
  - 5xx rate >= 2x baseline for 15 minutes (hosted inference)
  - timeouts/cancellations >= 2x baseline for 15 minutes
- Scheduler instability:
  - runaway queue depth (monotonic growth) for > 10 minutes with no recovery AND admission control not stabilizing.
- Telemetry incident:
  - metric series cardinality > agreed cap (see Observability spec) for > 10 minutes OR ingestion costs spike to paging threshold.

SEV-1 triggers (pause + revert canary)
- Standard tier p95 regression >= +15% for 60 minutes.
- TTFT regression for streaming routes >= +20% for 30 minutes.
- Increased 429/503 due to backpressure without corresponding improvement in tail latency.
- Dedicated: tenant reports override precedence wrong + reproducible (customer-impacting) (reference SUP-48271 class issues).

SEV-2 triggers (hold, investigate)
- Throughput/cost improvements not materializing (<= +2% tokens/sec) after 24h at steady traffic.
- Unexpected profile selection distribution (e.g., overload_mode_enabled > 1% for long periods; model_constraint_fallback frequent).
- Support ticket spike about “why did latency change?” even if metrics show small deltas (messaging/expectations risk).

Rollout steps (staged)
Step 0 — Dry run (staging)
- [ ] Enable tiered defaults in staging at 100% for at least 2 hours with synthetic load (mixed prompt lengths + streaming/non-streaming).
- [ ] Validate reason codes show expected distribution.
- [ ] Validate dashboards show tier breakdown.
- [ ] Validate kill switch and per-route disable path works.
Notes: staging is necessary but insufficient; primary risks show up only under real concurrency distributions.

Step 1 — Internal canary (prod, Redwood-owned traffic)
Scope
- Enable for Redwood internal routes only (dogfood) in 1 region.
- Target tier coverage: fast + standard only initially (leave cost-efficient off unless explicitly needed).
Checklist
- [ ] Feature flag on for internal canary cohort (confirm config commit hash).
- [ ] Confirm which models are eligible (top model families used by internal apps).
- [ ] Monitor for 2–4 hours:
  - p99/p95, TTFT, queue depth, overload counters
  - reason codes: ensure tier_default_applied dominates; check for unexpected model_constraint_fallback
Exit criteria
- [ ] No SEV-1/0 triggers.
- [ ] At least stable performance; minor regressions investigated/understood.

Step 2 — Hosted API subset (external, small %)
Scope
- Start at 1% of eligible traffic in 1 region (prefer region w/ good headroom).
- Eligible endpoints: chat + generate; exclude any known “fragile” routes (large context, unusual streaming patterns) until later.
Ramp plan (default)
- 1% → 5% → 10% → 25% → 50% → 100% (per region), one step per 24h if green.
Checklist per ramp
- [ ] Pre-ramp snapshot: baselines (24h) for latency/error/cost.
- [ ] Turn on ramp; annotate dashboards with change marker.
- [ ] Monitor first 30 minutes continuously; then hourly checks.
- [ ] Verify perf canary and alerts remain green.
- [ ] Review reason codes: ensure no silent override precedence issues.
Exit criteria per ramp
- [ ] Meets tier SLO targets per published definition.
- [ ] Error budget burn not elevated.
- [ ] Support tickets stable.

Step 3 — Dedicated opt-in (per tenant/cluster)
Scope
- Opt-in only, coordinated with Dedicated PM (Lauren Bishop) + CS.
- Start with 1–2 friendly tenants with mixed workloads.
Checklist
- [ ] Confirm per-tenant override precedence and UI/CLI path for settings.
- [ ] Validate “effective profile” introspection (Console shows what’s applied + why).
- [ ] Run tenant-specific observation window (min 48h) due to traffic variability.
- [ ] Confirm rollback path for tenant is immediate (tenant-level disable) without affecting other tenants.
Exit criteria
- [ ] No tenant-specific p95/p99 regressions beyond thresholds.
- [ ] Override precedence support questions answered; docs link provided.

Step 4 — Broader default enablement (Hosted)
Scope
- Expand to additional regions.
- Consider enabling cost-efficient tier defaults for eligible workloads after fast/standard stable.
Checklist
- [ ] Ensure capacity planning OK (cost-efficient may increase batch size; watch queue blowups).
- [ ] Re-validate telemetry cardinality across multi-region.
- [ ] Confirm docs and SDK tier parameter released (python/typescript) if required for customers.

Operational runbook: kill switch + rollback
Immediate kill switch (global)
- Owner on call: SRE (Rafael) or Runtime on call (Scheduling).
- Action: flip global flag “tiered_batching_defaults_enabled=false” (control plane).
- Expected effect: revert to legacy/manual batching behavior (or previous default behavior) within propagation window.
- Follow-up: postmortem-style notes in incident channel if SEV-0.

Scoped rollback (recommended first when possible)
- Disable for:
  - specific region
  - specific model family
  - specific route
  - specific Dedicated tenant
- Use when impact is localized or model-specific.

Rollback validation checklist
- [ ] Confirm reason codes switch from tier_default_applied to legacy/disabled (or explicit “disabled” code).
- [ ] Confirm latency returns toward baseline (allow 10–20 min for queues to drain).
- [ ] Confirm queue depth normalizes.
- [ ] Confirm error rates normalize.

Comms templates (copy/paste)
1) Internal (Slack #eng-runtime / #eng-sre)
Subject: [Rollout] tiered batching defaults — ramp to <X%> in <region>
Body:
- Change: enable tiered batching defaults (runtime 1.22) at <X%> for eligible hosted traffic in <region>
- Start time: <UTC timestamp>
- Owners: SRE <name>, Runtime <name>
- Dashboards: <links>
- Abort criteria: SEV-0/1 per rollout checklist; kill switch ready
- Notes: watch fast p99 + streaming TTFT; watch metric cardinality

2) Customer support macro (if customers ask “why did latency/cost change?”)
Thanks for reaching out — we’re rolling out updated batching defaults that map to our latency SLO tiers (fast/standard/cost-efficient). This change only affects runtime scheduling; it does not change data handling/logging. If you share:
- route name / model
- streaming vs non-streaming
- region
- timeframe + request IDs
we can confirm the effective tier/profile and suggest either (a) a tier selection, or (b) an override if needed.

3) Status page (only if customer-facing impact)
Investigating elevated latency for a subset of inference requests in <region>. We’ve paused a recent runtime configuration change and are rolling back to prior settings. Next update in <30 min>.

4) Direct email to Dedicated opt-in tenant (pre-change)
We’re enabling tiered batching defaults (fast/standard/cost-efficient) for your Dedicated cluster as an opt-in performance/cost feature. We will start with <tier> and monitor p95/p99 latency and throughput. You can override or disable at any time; we’ll share a Console screenshot and the exact config key. No changes to logging/retention or data access.

Known sharp edges / “gotchas” to watch
- Metric cardinality blow-up if tier/model/route labels combine without sampling/limits.
- Mixed streaming + long prompts: starvation risk if wait budgets too tight; watch fast tier carefully.
- Overload behavior: make sure we’re not “solving” overload by just increasing batching (tail latency can worsen).
- Dedicated overrides: precedence confusion is common; ensure Console shows effective config and reason code.

Open TODOs (as of last_modified)
- [ ] Add explicit link to overload behavior runbook (ENG-18433) once published.
- [ ] Decide paging thresholds: fast-tier p99 regression (debate: page vs ticket) — see #eng-sre 1768523309.
- [ ] Finalize ramp schedule per region (coordinate w/ fleet readiness).
- [ ] Add “baseline refresh” process snippet for perf canary (INT-33055).

Change log notes (quick)
- 2026-03-08: initial checklist drafted from SRE + runtime threads.
- 2026-03-18: added telemetry/cardinality abort criteria after early canary spike discussions.
- 2026-04-24: updated staged ramp steps + comms templates; aligned with docs readiness email.

source.title: Game Day Plan: Auto-rollback on SLO breach (rollout controller integration)
source.content:
Owner: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
Primary objectives:
- Validate that the rollout controller takes safe, automated action (pause/rollback) when rollout-scoped SLOs breach.
- Validate that suppression/override and "known incident" handling works and is auditable.
- Validate monitor staleness/missing-data behavior to prevent both false rollbacks and unsafe fail-open behavior.
- Validate dashboards/alerts + incident-bot message quality for oncall.

Scope of this game day
- Environments: staging (required), one internal production route (optional, only after staging pass + SRE sign-off)
- Rollout types covered:
  1) Canary rollout (staged traffic percentages)
  2) A/B rollout (split between baseline and candidate)
- Signals covered:
  - Latency SLO burn-rate (p95 and p99 where available)
  - Error-rate SLO burn-rate (5xx)
  - Saturation signal (optional for this game day; we’ll treat it as “informational” unless policy enables it)
- Non-goals:
  - Multi-region failover orchestration beyond rollback/pause
  - Quality-eval-based rollback (handled in separate project)

Participants (expected)
- SRE: Rafael Mendes (facilitator), Sean Gallagher (SLO semantics), Aisha Bello (dashboards/alerts)
- Eng Platform: Jada Williams (rollout controller), Jordan Lee (policy eval), Benji Okafor (policy config)
- Console: Chloe Martin (Console API), Felix Schneider (audit/UI)
- Release/QA: Leila Farouk (E2E), Connor O’Brien (release checklist)

Pre-req checklist (must be green 24h prior)
1) Feature flags
- Auto-rollback integration flag enabled in staging
- Separate flags for: “pause-only mode” vs “rollback enabled” documented and confirmed
- Incident-bot Slack notification enabled for staging channel (notify-only; do not page)

2) Monitors + dashboards
- Rollout-scoped SLO monitors exist for the target route/model/version/region/tier
- Burn-rate endpoint returns:
  - status (ok/breaching)
  - fast-window + slow-window values
  - sample size / request volume
  - timestamp of last datapoint (for staleness detection)
- Grafana dashboard “Auto-rollback events” live and accessible to game day participants
- Alert rules configured for:
  - auto action triggered
  - rollback suppressed
  - monitor missing/stale

3) Safety / guardrails
- Minimum sample size gating enabled (confirm exact numbers used in staging)
- Debounce/holdoff enabled (confirm holdoff duration)
- Rollback executor idempotency verified in staging (no double rollback)
- Rate-limited traffic shift enabled (no immediate 100% cutover in a single step)

4) Audit trail
- Audit events emitted to event stream (pause/rollback/suppress) with correlation id
- Console endpoint can fetch auto-actions for a rollout id
- Retention expectations understood for staging (OK if shorter than prod)

5) Communication
- Dedicated Slack channel for game day: #eng-sre (thread pinned)
- Single “stop” word: “HALT” to pause the exercise if risk emerges

Definitions used during exercise
- Candidate: model version being rolled out
- Baseline: last known-good model version
- Auto action types:
  - PAUSE: stop the rollout from advancing; keep traffic split as-is (or optionally shift a small amount back to baseline depending on policy)
  - ROLLBACK: shift traffic back to baseline according to executor safety steps; mark rollout as rolled back
  - SUPPRESS: do not take auto action for a period, but record decision + reason

Success criteria (global)
A) Correctness
- When we inject a real breach (latency or 5xx), the controller takes the expected action within the expected time budget.
- When we inject non-breach or insufficient samples, the controller does NOT rollback.

B) Safety
- No oscillation (no repeated rollback/pause loop) under flapping signals due to debounce/holdoff.
- Rollback actions are idempotent (retries do not cause unsafe traffic behavior).

C) Operability
- Audit trail explains “why” (policy version, monitor ids, windows, key metric snapshot refs).
- Dashboards reflect events within ~2 minutes of action.
- Slack notification is understandable and includes: rollout id, route, region, action, reason, and links.

Target timing budgets (staging)
- Detection: breach should be observed by evaluator within 1 evaluation cycle (target <= 60–90s after sustained breach begins, depending on window config)
- Decision + action initiation: <= 30s after detection
- Traffic shift completion for rollback: <= 5 minutes (rate-limited stepped shift)

Test plan overview
We’ll run 3 primary scenarios and 2 optional edge cases.

Scenario 1: Latency spike during canary (p95/p99)
Goal: verify fast-burn + slow-burn logic, minimum sample size gating, and rollback vs pause policy.

Setup
- Choose staging route: /v1/chat.completions (staging) with a known stable baseline model and a candidate model version.
- Rollout type: canary (10% -> 25% -> 50% steps)
- Policy: enable auto action for latency SLO. For this scenario we want rollback enabled (not just pause-only), unless we are still in MVP “pause-only” mode; if pause-only, expected result is PAUSE.

Fault injection approach (pick one)
- Preferred: synthetic latency injection in candidate path only (e.g., per-request delay at runtime layer or proxy for candidate model version)
- Alternate: force a candidate configuration change known to increase latency (only if reversible and safe in staging)

Steps
1) Start rollout, advance to 25% candidate.
2) Begin latency injection for candidate traffic only.
3) Observe burn-rate status: fast window should breach first; slow window should breach if sustained.
4) Confirm evaluator logs show:
   - monitor ids queried
   - sample size check
   - breach confirmation across required windows
5) Confirm controller action:
   - If policy says rollback on confirmed breach: rollback executor begins shifting back to baseline
   - If policy says pause first: rollout pauses; traffic remains at current step (or per-policy small shift back)
6) Confirm executor behavior:
   - traffic shift occurs in steps (rate limited)
   - action is idempotent if the controller retries
7) Stop injection and confirm system returns to normal.

Expected outcome
- Auto action triggered after sustained breach and after sample-size gate.
- One and only one action for the breach (no repeated triggers during holdoff).

Validation points
- Audit event contains: action=ROLLBACK or PAUSE, reason=latency_burn_rate, windows used, sample sizes, policy version.
- Console shows auto action in rollout timeline within 2 minutes.
- Slack notification posted once, with links to dashboard + rollout.

Pass/Fail
- PASS if action taken within timing budget and audit + dashboards are correct.
- FAIL if:
  - no action is taken for a real sustained breach
  - rollback triggers with insufficient samples
  - traffic shift behaves unsafely or repeats


Scenario 2: 5xx burst during A/B test
Goal: verify error-rate SLO integration and “shift back to baseline” semantics for A/B.

Setup
- Rollout type: A/B split (e.g., 50/50 baseline/candidate)
- Policy: error-rate SLO triggers action. Prefer rollback (shift traffic back to baseline) rather than pause.

Fault injection approach
- Preferred: inject 5xx responses in candidate only (runtime fault injector, or a controlled failure path)
- Ensure we can stop injection instantly.

Steps
1) Start A/B rollout and hold at 50/50.
2) Begin error injection on candidate.
3) Observe SLO burn-rate for error-rate crossing thresholds.
4) Confirm evaluator sees breach and chooses action per policy.
5) Confirm action execution:
   - shift traffic back to baseline over stepped intervals
   - candidate traffic reduces to 0% (or policy-defined minimum) and stays there

Expected outcome
- Rollback action results in stable baseline traffic with error rate returning to normal.

Validation points
- Monitor query scopes include rollout labels (route + model version + region + tier).
- Audit event includes error-rate metrics snapshot reference.
- Alerts: notify-only in staging, no paging storms.

Pass/Fail
- PASS if traffic shifts back and errors resolve; no repeated flapping.


Scenario 3: Monitor staleness / missing data
Goal: validate staleness detection, fail-safe posture, and “monitor missing/stale” surfacing.

Setup
- Use any rollout type (canary is fine).
- Ensure monitor adapter exposes last datapoint timestamp.

Fault injection approach
- Option A: break the monitor query endpoint for the specific monitor id (simulate provider partial outage)
- Option B: stop metrics ingestion for the relevant labels (simulate missing time series)
- Option C: return stale timestamps from adapter (simulate lag)

Steps
1) Start rollout and move to a step where auto-eval is active.
2) Induce staleness/missing monitor state.
3) Confirm evaluator behavior according to policy:
   - Should NOT rollback based on missing data.
   - Should either:
     a) PAUSE rollout (conservative) OR
     b) SUPPRESS auto actions but raise an explicit alert/event
   (confirm which is implemented in current build; document here after dry run)
4) Confirm surfacing:
   - Audit event: action=SUPPRESS or PAUSE with reason=monitor_stale or monitor_missing
   - Dashboard panel increments “monitor missing/stale” counter
   - Slack notification includes “monitor stale” and suggests manual verification steps

Expected outcome
- No rollback based purely on missing data.
- Operator-facing signal is clear and actionable.

Pass/Fail
- PASS if the system behaves consistently with documented stance and provides clear visibility.
- FAIL if it either silently does nothing (no audit/event) OR rolls back due to missing data.


Optional scenario 4: Flapping latency (debounce/holdoff)
Goal: ensure debounce prevents oscillation and no repeated actions.
Steps
- Alternate 2–3 minute latency injection on/off to create intermittent fast-window breaches.
Expected
- No immediate rollback unless breach persists long enough to satisfy both windows and debounce rules.
Validation
- Ensure evaluator logs show “debounced / holdoff active” and action not repeatedly triggered.


Optional scenario 5: Admin suppression + justification
Goal: validate override path is gated and auditable.
Steps
1) Start a rollout with auto-rollback enabled.
2) Apply suppression window via admin API/control.
3) Trigger a real breach.
Expected
- No rollback occurs while suppression active.
- Audit event records who suppressed, justification, TTL.

Operational notes / what to capture during the exercise
- Rollout id(s), route, region, tier
- Policy version/hash used
- Monitor ids queried (latency, error)
- Timestamps: start injection, breach observed, action decided, rollback started, rollback completed
- Links: Grafana panels, Console rollout page, relevant logs

Oncall simulation (lightweight)
- Assign a “mock oncall” (Sean) to interpret only the dashboards + Slack message.
- Success if mock oncall can answer:
  1) What happened?
  2) What action was taken automatically?
  3) Is it safe now?
  4) What do we do next (retry rollout vs investigate)?

Risks / safety controls
- Staging only for fault injection unless explicitly approved.
- If we attempt internal production route:
  - must be a low-risk internal route
  - must be notify-only (no paging)
  - must have pre-approved backout plan
- “HALT” if:
  - unexpected broad impact
  - rollback executor appears unstable
  - repeated triggers suggest flapping bug

Post-game day outputs
- Game day notes: what passed/failed, timing data, screenshots/links
- Bugs filed in Linear with severity and suggested owners
- Decision: readiness for staged enablement expansion (yes/no + conditions)

Open questions (to resolve before final run)
1) For stale/missing monitors, do we PAUSE by default or SUPPRESS with alert? (Implementation may currently do one; need final stance.)
2) When PAUSE triggers, do we also shift a small percentage back to baseline (e.g., step-down) or hold traffic constant?
3) Minimum sample size defaults in staging: what are they, and do they match SRE recommended safe bounds?
4) Which alerts page vs notify in production? (For game day, keep staging notify-only.)

Appendix: quick reference
- Expected Slack notification fields: action, rollout id, route, model baseline->candidate, region, tier, reason (latency/error/stale), dashboard link, Console link
- Audit event must include: acting service, policy version, monitor ids, window config, sample sizes, staleness status, suppression details (if any)
- Dashboard panels to verify:
  - Auto actions over time
  - Suppressions over time
  - Monitor staleness/missing
  - Rollout health by route/model/region

Steps
1) Start rollout, advance to 25% candidate.
2) Begin latency injection for candidate traffic only.

4) Confirm evaluator logs show:

   - If policy says rollback on confirmed breach: rollback executor begins shifting back to baseline

- Console shows auto action in rollout timeline within 2 minutes.

- PASS if action taken within timing budget and audit + dashboards are correct.
- FAIL if:
  - no action is taken for a real sustained breach
  - rollback triggers with insufficient samples
  - traffic shift behaves unsafely or repeats


Scenario 2: 5xx burst during A/B test
Goal: verify error-rate SLO integration and “shift back to baseline” semantics for A/B.

Setup
- Rollout type: A/B split (e.g., 50/50 baseline/candidate)
- Policy: error-rate SLO triggers action. Prefer rollback (shift traffic back to baseline) rather than pause.

- Preferred: inject 5xx responses in candidate only (runtime fault injector, or a controlled failure path)
- Ensure we can stop injection instantly.

Steps
1) Start A/B rollout and hold at 50/50.
2) Begin error injection on candidate.
3) Observe SLO burn-rate for error-rate crossing thresholds.
4) Confirm evaluator sees breach and chooses action per policy.
5) Confirm action execution:
   - shift traffic back to baseline over stepped intervals
   - candidate traffic reduces to 0% (or policy-defined minimum) and stays there

Expected outcome
- Rollback action results in stable baseline traffic with error rate returning to normal.

Validation points
- Monitor query scopes include rollout labels (route + model version + region + tier).
- Audit event includes error-rate metrics snapshot reference.
- Alerts: notify-only in staging, no paging storms.

- PASS if traffic shifts back and errors resolve; no repeated flapping.


Scenario 3: Monitor staleness / missing data
Goal: validate staleness detection, fail-safe posture, and “monitor missing/stale” surfacing.

Setup
- Use any rollout type (canary is fine).
- Ensure monitor adapter exposes last datapoint timestamp.

Fault injection approach
- Option A: break the monitor query endpoint for the specific monitor id (simulate provider partial outage)
- Option B: stop metrics ingestion for the relevant labels (simulate missing time series)
- Option C: return stale timestamps from adapter (simulate lag)

Steps
1) Start rollout and move to a step where auto-eval is active.
2) Induce staleness/missing monitor state.
3) Confirm evaluator behavior according to policy:
   - Should NOT rollback based on missing data.
   - Should either:
     a) PAUSE rollout (conservative) OR
     b) SUPPRESS auto actions but raise an explicit alert/event
   (confirm which is implemented in current build; document here after dry run)
4) Confirm surfacing:
   - Audit event: action=SUPPRESS or PAUSE with reason=monitor_stale or monitor_missing
   - Dashboard panel increments “monitor missing/stale” counter
   - Slack notification includes “monitor stale” and suggests manual verification steps

Expected outcome
- No rollback based purely on missing data.
- Operator-facing signal is clear and actionable.

Pass/Fail
- PASS if the system behaves consistently with documented stance and provides clear visibility.
- FAIL if it either silently does nothing (no audit/event) OR rolls back due to missing data.


Optional scenario 4: Flapping latency (debounce/holdoff)
Goal: ensure debounce prevents oscillation and no repeated actions.
Steps
- Alternate 2–3 minute latency injection on/off to create intermittent fast-window breaches.
Expected
- No immediate rollback unless breach persists long enough to satisfy both windows and debounce rules.
Validation
- Ensure evaluator logs show “debounced / holdoff active” and action not repeatedly triggered.


Optional scenario 5: Admin suppression + justification
Goal: validate override path is gated and auditable.
Steps
1) Start a rollout with auto-rollback enabled.
2) Apply suppression window via admin API/control.
3) Trigger a real breach.
Expected
- No rollback occurs while suppression active.
- Audit event records who suppressed, justification, TTL.

Operational notes / what to capture during the exercise
- Rollout id(s), route, region, tier
- Policy version/hash used
- Monitor ids queried (latency, error)
- Timestamps: start injection, breach observed, action decided, rollback started, rollback completed
- Links: Grafana panels, Console rollout page, relevant logs

Oncall simulation (lightweight)
- Assign a “mock oncall” (Sean) to interpret only the dashboards + Slack message.
- Success if mock oncall can answer:
  1) What happened?
  2) What action was taken automatically?
  3) Is it safe now?
  4) What do we do next (retry rollout vs investigate)?

Risks / safety controls
- Staging only for fault injection unless explicitly approved.
- If we attempt internal production route:
  - must be a low-risk internal route
  - must be notify-only (no paging)
  - must have pre-approved backout plan
- “HALT” if:
  - unexpected broad impact
  - rollback executor appears unstable
  - repeated triggers suggest flapping bug

Post-game day outputs
- Game day notes: what passed/failed, timing data, screenshots/links
- Bugs filed in Linear with severity and suggested owners
- Decision: readiness for staged enablement expansion (yes/no + conditions)

Open questions (to resolve before final run)
1) For stale/missing monitors, do we PAUSE by default or SUPPRESS with alert? (Implementation may currently do one; need final stance.)
2) When PAUSE triggers, do we also shift a small percentage back to baseline (e.g., step-down) or hold traffic constant?
3) Minimum sample size defaults in staging: what are they, and do they match SRE recommended safe bounds?
4) Which alerts page vs notify in production? (For game day, keep staging notify-only.)

Appendix: quick reference
- Expected Slack notification fields: action, rollout id, route, model baseline->candidate, region, tier, reason (latency/error/stale), dashboard link, Console link
- Audit event must include: acting service, policy version, monitor ids, window config, sample sizes, staleness status, suppression details (if any)
- Dashboard panels to verify:
  - Auto actions over time
  - Suppressions over time
  - Monitor staleness/missing
  - Rollout health by route/model/region
text:
Owner: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
Primary objectives:
- Validate that the rollout controller takes safe, automated action (pause/rollback) when rollout-scoped SLOs breach.
- Validate that suppression/override and "known incident" handling works and is auditable.
- Validate monitor staleness/missing-data behavior to prevent both false rollbacks and unsafe fail-open behavior.
- Validate dashboards/alerts + incident-bot message quality for oncall.

Scope of this game day
- Environments: staging (required), one internal production route (optional, only after staging pass + SRE sign-off)
- Rollout types covered:
  1) Canary rollout (staged traffic percentages)
  2) A/B rollout (split between baseline and candidate)
- Signals covered:
  - Latency SLO burn-rate (p95 and p99 where available)
  - Error-rate SLO burn-rate (5xx)
  - Saturation signal (optional for this game day; we’ll treat it as “informational” unless policy enables it)
- Non-goals:
  - Multi-region failover orchestration beyond rollback/pause
  - Quality-eval-based rollback (handled in separate project)

Participants (expected)
- SRE: Rafael Mendes (facilitator), Sean Gallagher (SLO semantics), Aisha Bello (dashboards/alerts)
- Eng Platform: Jada Williams (rollout controller), Jordan Lee (policy eval), Benji Okafor (policy config)
- Console: Chloe Martin (Console API), Felix Schneider (audit/UI)
- Release/QA: Leila Farouk (E2E), Connor O’Brien (release checklist)

Pre-req checklist (must be green 24h prior)
1) Feature flags
- Auto-rollback integration flag enabled in staging
- Separate flags for: “pause-only mode” vs “rollback enabled” documented and confirmed
- Incident-bot Slack notification enabled for staging channel (notify-only; do not page)

2) Monitors + dashboards
- Rollout-scoped SLO monitors exist for the target route/model/version/region/tier
- Burn-rate endpoint returns:
  - status (ok/breaching)
  - fast-window + slow-window values
  - sample size / request volume
  - timestamp of last datapoint (for staleness detection)
- Grafana dashboard “Auto-rollback events” live and accessible to game day participants
- Alert rules configured for:
  - auto action triggered
  - rollback suppressed
  - monitor missing/stale

3) Safety / guardrails
- Minimum sample size gating enabled (confirm exact numbers used in staging)
- Debounce/holdoff enabled (confirm holdoff duration)
- Rollback executor idempotency verified in staging (no double rollback)
- Rate-limited traffic shift enabled (no immediate 100% cutover in a single step)

4) Audit trail
- Audit events emitted to event stream (pause/rollback/suppress) with correlation id
- Console endpoint can fetch auto-actions for a rollout id
- Retention expectations understood for staging (OK if shorter than prod)

5) Communication
- Dedicated Slack channel for game day: #eng-sre (thread pinned)
- Single “stop” word: “HALT” to pause the exercise if risk emerges

Definitions used during exercise
- Candidate: model version being rolled out
- Baseline: last known-good model version
- Auto action types:
  - PAUSE: stop the rollout from advancing; keep traffic split as-is (or optionally shift a small amount back to baseline depending on policy)
  - ROLLBACK: shift traffic back to baseline according to executor safety steps; mark rollout as rolled back
  - SUPPRESS: do not take auto action for a period, but record decision + reason

Success criteria (global)
A) Correctness
- When we inject a real breach (latency or 5xx), the controller takes the expected action within the expected time budget.
- When we inject non-breach or insufficient samples, the controller does NOT rollback.

B) Safety
- No oscillation (no repeated rollback/pause loop) under flapping signals due to debounce/holdoff.
- Rollback actions are idempotent (retries do not cause unsafe traffic behavior).

C) Operability
- Audit trail explains “why” (policy version, monitor ids, windows, key metric snapshot refs).
- Dashboards reflect events within ~2 minutes of action.
- Slack notification is understandable and includes: rollout id, route, region, action, reason, and links.

Target timing budgets (staging)
- Detection: breach should be observed by evaluator within 1 evaluation cycle (target <= 60–90s after sustained breach begins, depending on window config)
- Decision + action initiation: <= 30s after detection
- Traffic shift completion for rollback: <= 5 minutes (rate-limited stepped shift)

Test plan overview
We’ll run 3 primary scenarios and 2 optional edge cases.

Scenario 1: Latency spike during canary (p95/p99)
Goal: verify fast-burn + slow-burn logic, minimum sample size gating, and rollback vs pause policy.

Setup
- Choose staging route: /v1/chat.completions (staging) with a known stable baseline model and a candidate model version.
- Rollout type: canary (10% -> 25% -> 50% steps)
- Policy: enable auto action for latency SLO. For this scenario we want rollback enabled (not just pause-only), unless we are still in MVP “pause-only” mode; if pause-only, expected result is PAUSE.

Fault injection approach (pick one)
- Preferred: synthetic latency injection in candidate path only (e.g., per-request delay at runtime layer or proxy for candidate model version)
- Alternate: force a candidate configuration change known to increase latency (only if reversible and safe in staging)

Steps
1) Start rollout, advance to 25% candidate.
2) Begin latency injection for candidate traffic only.
3) Observe burn-rate status: fast window should breach first; slow window should breach if sustained.
4) Confirm evaluator logs show:
   - monitor ids queried
   - sample size check
   - breach confirmation across required windows
5) Confirm controller action:
   - If policy says rollback on confirmed breach: rollback executor begins shifting back to baseline
   - If policy says pause first: rollout pauses; traffic remains at current step (or per-policy small shift back)
6) Confirm executor behavior:
   - traffic shift occurs in steps (rate limited)
   - action is idempotent if the controller retries
7) Stop injection and confirm system returns to normal.

Expected outcome
- Auto action triggered after sustained breach and after sample-size gate.
- One and only one action for the breach (no repeated triggers during holdoff).

Validation points
- Audit event contains: action=ROLLBACK or PAUSE, reason=latency_burn_rate, windows used, sample sizes, policy version.
- Console shows auto action in rollout timeline within 2 minutes.
- Slack notification posted once, with links to dashboard + rollout.

Pass/Fail
- PASS if action taken within timing budget and audit + dashboards are correct.
- FAIL if:
  - no action is taken for a real sustained breach
  - rollback triggers with insufficient samples
  - traffic shift behaves unsafely or repeats


Scenario 2: 5xx burst during A/B test
Goal: verify error-rate SLO integration and “shift back to baseline” semantics for A/B.

Setup
- Rollout type: A/B split (e.g., 50/50 baseline/candidate)
- Policy: error-rate SLO triggers action. Prefer rollback (shift traffic back to baseline) rather than pause.

Fault injection approach
- Preferred: inject 5xx responses in candidate only (runtime fault injector, or a controlled failure path)
- Ensure we can stop injection instantly.

Steps
1) Start A/B rollout and hold at 50/50.
2) Begin error injection on candidate.
3) Observe SLO burn-rate for error-rate crossing thresholds.
4) Confirm evaluator sees breach and chooses action per policy.
5) Confirm action execution:
   - shift traffic back to baseline over stepped intervals
   - candidate traffic reduces to 0% (or policy-defined minimum) and stays there

Expected outcome
- Rollback action results in stable baseline traffic with error rate returning to normal.

Validation points
- Monitor query scopes include rollout labels (route + model version + region + tier).
- Audit event includes error-rate metrics snapshot reference.
- Alerts: notify-only in staging, no paging storms.

Pass/Fail
- PASS if traffic shifts back and errors resolve; no repeated flapping.


Scenario 3: Monitor staleness / missing data
Goal: validate staleness detection, fail-safe posture, and “monitor missing/stale” surfacing.

Setup
- Use any rollout type (canary is fine).
- Ensure monitor adapter exposes last datapoint timestamp.

Fault injection approach
- Option A: break the monitor query endpoint for the specific monitor id (simulate provider partial outage)
- Option B: stop metrics ingestion for the relevant labels (simulate missing time series)
- Option C: return stale timestamps from adapter (simulate lag)

Steps
1) Start rollout and move to a step where auto-eval is active.
2) Induce staleness/missing monitor state.
3) Confirm evaluator behavior according to policy:
   - Should NOT rollback based on missing data.
   - Should either:
     a) PAUSE rollout (conservative) OR
     b) SUPPRESS auto actions but raise an explicit alert/event
   (confirm which is implemented in current build; document here after dry run)
4) Confirm surfacing:
   - Audit event: action=SUPPRESS or PAUSE with reason=monitor_stale or monitor_missing
   - Dashboard panel increments “monitor missing/stale” counter
   - Slack notification includes “monitor stale” and suggests manual verification steps

Expected outcome
- No rollback based purely on missing data.
- Operator-facing signal is clear and actionable.

Pass/Fail
- PASS if the system behaves consistently with documented stance and provides clear visibility.
- FAIL if it either silently does nothing (no audit/event) OR rolls back due to missing data.


Optional scenario 4: Flapping latency (debounce/holdoff)
Goal: ensure debounce prevents oscillation and no repeated actions.
Steps
- Alternate 2–3 minute latency injection on/off to create intermittent fast-window breaches.
Expected
- No immediate rollback unless breach persists long enough to satisfy both windows and debounce rules.
Validation
- Ensure evaluator logs show “debounced / holdoff active” and action not repeatedly triggered.


Optional scenario 5: Admin suppression + justification
Goal: validate override path is gated and auditable.
Steps
1) Start a rollout with auto-rollback enabled.
2) Apply suppression window via admin API/control.
3) Trigger a real breach.
Expected
- No rollback occurs while suppression active.
- Audit event records who suppressed, justification, TTL.

Operational notes / what to capture during the exercise
- Rollout id(s), route, region, tier
- Policy version/hash used
- Monitor ids queried (latency, error)
- Timestamps: start injection, breach observed, action decided, rollback started, rollback completed
- Links: Grafana panels, Console rollout page, relevant logs

Oncall simulation (lightweight)
- Assign a “mock oncall” (Sean) to interpret only the dashboards + Slack message.
- Success if mock oncall can answer:
  1) What happened?
  2) What action was taken automatically?
  3) Is it safe now?
  4) What do we do next (retry rollout vs investigate)?

Risks / safety controls
- Staging only for fault injection unless explicitly approved.
- If we attempt internal production route:
  - must be a low-risk internal route
  - must be notify-only (no paging)
  - must have pre-approved backout plan
- “HALT” if:
  - unexpected broad impact
  - rollback executor appears unstable
  - repeated triggers suggest flapping bug

Post-game day outputs
- Game day notes: what passed/failed, timing data, screenshots/links
- Bugs filed in Linear with severity and suggested owners
- Decision: readiness for staged enablement expansion (yes/no + conditions)

Open questions (to resolve before final run)
1) For stale/missing monitors, do we PAUSE by default or SUPPRESS with alert? (Implementation may currently do one; need final stance.)
2) When PAUSE triggers, do we also shift a small percentage back to baseline (e.g., step-down) or hold traffic constant?
3) Minimum sample size defaults in staging: what are they, and do they match SRE recommended safe bounds?
4) Which alerts page vs notify in production? (For game day, keep staging notify-only.)

Appendix: quick reference
- Expected Slack notification fields: action, rollout id, route, model baseline->candidate, region, tier, reason (latency/error/stale), dashboard link, Console link
- Audit event must include: acting service, policy version, monitor ids, window config, sample sizes, staleness status, suppression details (if any)
- Dashboard panels to verify:
  - Auto actions over time
  - Suppressions over time
  - Monitor staleness/missing
  - Rollout health by route/model/region

source.title: Perf Canary Alert Policy (Drafts): warning vs paging, dampening, routing, escalation
source.content:
Doc purpose
- Working draft of how we want perf-canary alerts to behave in prod (WARN vs PAGE), how we dampen/dedupe to avoid alert storms, and who we route/escalate to.
- This is intentionally a “policy draft” (not a formal Confluence page yet). Expect edits as we learn from phase-1 rollout.

TL;DR (current direction)
- Default to WARNING for most models/regions.
- Paging only for “top models” (by customer impact) AND only on sustained regressions that pass minimum sample size and confidence rules.
- Dampen aggressively: require persistence, don’t page on single-window spikes, add cooldowns.
- Every alert must include: baseline ref (versioned), observed window stats, attribution hints (queueing vs runtime-only, kernel tag, runtime build, GPU SKU), dashboard + runbook links.

Definitions and assumptions
- Canary sends synthetic requests with fixed workload profiles (prompt length buckets + prefill/decode mix). No customer data.
- Baselines are stored per {model_id, model_version, runtime_build?, kernel_tag?, gpu_sku, region} with explicit versioning. For alerting we pick the “active baseline” for the canary config.
- Regressions we care about (primary):
  - p95 end-to-end latency up
  - tokens/sec down
- Secondary signals for attribution (not paging on these alone initially):
  - runtime-only p95 latency
  - scheduler queueing time p95
  - prefill vs decode breakdown
  - batch size distribution shifts
  - KV cache hit-rate changes (if emitted; not required for MVP)

Metric conventions (names are tentative / for queries)
- perf_canary_request_latency_ms{quantile="0.95",model,model_version,region,tier,gpu_sku,runtime_build,kernel_tag}
- perf_canary_runtime_latency_ms{quantile="0.95",...}
- perf_canary_queueing_latency_ms{quantile="0.95",...}
- perf_canary_tokens_per_second{model,model_version,region,tier,gpu_sku,runtime_build,kernel_tag}
- perf_canary_requests_total{model,...,status="ok|err"}
- perf_canary_baseline_id{model,...} (or exposed via annotation field in alert payload)

Alert classes
1) WARNING (non-paging) — “Heads up, investigate during business hours; validate if real and if tied to a rollout.”
2) PAGE (Sev2 default; Sev1 only if broad/top-tier + persistent + clear user impact risk)

Note: We should avoid “Sev1” for perf-only until we have more confidence in signal fidelity and a clean mitigation path.

Routing targets (current)
- WARNING:
  - Slack: #eng-runtime
  - FYI tag: #eng-platform (if it looks like telemetry/baseline issue)
- PAGE:
  - On-call page: SRE primary (per region rotation)
  - Slack incident thread created by incident-bot in #incidents with auto-mentions:
    - @oncall-sre
    - @oncall-runtime (secondary)
  - If regression correlates to active release window, auto-mention #eng-releases as FYI (not action owner unless we need a freeze).

Model tiers (policy input)
We need an explicit “canary_impact_tier” per model in control plane config.
- Tier A (Top models): eligible for paging once tuned; must be stable in warning for ~1 week.
- Tier B (Important): warning-only unless we see repeated true positives.
- Tier C (Coverage / high-risk changes): warning-only; primarily for release confidence.

Decision notes: page vs warn
- Why not page for all models?
  - Noise risk + on-call load + not all regressions are user-visible (e.g., canary profile mismatch or isolated SKU).
- What do we page on?
  - Only Tier A models OR regressions that strongly suggest imminent user impact (e.g., runtime-only latency up across multiple regions/SKUs).
- Tokens/sec drop vs p95 latency up:
  - Tokens/sec is useful but can be more sensitive to batching policy shifts and decode length distribution. For now, tokens/sec pages require higher confidence/persistence than latency.
- Queueing time:
  - Queueing changes can indicate capacity/fleet issues. However, queueing can also move due to autoscaling churn. We treat queueing as an attribution hint and a separate warning, not a standalone page trigger initially.

Core alert conditions (proposed)
All alerts require:
- min_samples: perf_canary_requests_total{status="ok"} >= N in the evaluation window
- error_rate guardrail: perf_canary_requests_total{status="err"} / total <= 1% (otherwise route to availability/error alert policy; different runbook)
- baseline available and “baseline_age_ok” (see baseline drift section)

Recommended minimum sample sizes (initial)
- For each (model, region, gpu_sku, tier):
  - N_warn = 120 requests per 10m window (or equivalent) for p95 stability
  - N_page = 300 requests aggregated over longer windows (see below)
Notes:
- We can lower N for very expensive models if we extend window length, but avoid windows > 60m for first pass (too slow to react).

WARNING triggers (Tier A/B/C)
A) p95 end-to-end latency regression (warning)
- Condition: p95_e2e_ms >= baseline_p95_e2e_ms * (1 + warn_latency_pct)
- Suggested warn_latency_pct:
  - Tier A: +8%
  - Tier B: +10%
  - Tier C: +12%
- Windowing: evaluate 10m windows, require 2 of last 3 windows to exceed threshold.
- Additional annotation fields:
  - runtime_only_delta_pct
  - queueing_delta_pct
  - kernel_tag, runtime_build, gpu_sku

B) tokens/sec regression (warning)
- Condition: tps <= baseline_tps * (1 - warn_tps_pct)
- Suggested warn_tps_pct:
  - Tier A: -8%
  - Tier B: -10%
  - Tier C: -12%
- Windowing: 10m windows, require 2 of last 3 windows.

PAGE triggers (Tier A only; gated rollout)
Pre-reqs to enable paging for a model:
- At least 7 days in warning-only with <2 false positives/week
- Baseline stable and updated at least once post-deploy per {region, gpu_sku} we page on
- Runbook validated in on-call training (tabletop)

P1) Sustained p95 latency regression (page)
- Condition: p95_e2e_ms >= baseline_p95_e2e_ms * (1 + page_latency_pct)
- Suggested page_latency_pct:
  - Tier A: +12% sustained
- Persistence / dampening:
  - Evaluate 15m windows; require 3 of last 4 windows over threshold (i.e., ~45-60m sustained)
  - Cooldown: once paged, don’t page again for same (model, region, sku) for 2 hours unless severity increases by +10% relative to last page
- Multi-dimension logic:
  - If regression is present across >=2 regions OR across >=2 GPU SKUs in same region, escalate confidence and shorten persistence requirement to 2 of last 3 windows.

P2) Sustained tokens/sec regression (page) — optional / later
- Condition: tps <= baseline_tps * (1 - page_tps_pct)
- Suggested page_tps_pct:
  - Tier A: -15% sustained
- Same persistence/cooldown as P1, but only if p95 latency is also degraded OR runtime-only latency increased.
Rationale: isolated tps drops without latency impact have historically been less actionable for SRE on short timescales.

Severity mapping (PAGE)
- Sev2 (default): single region OR single SKU; sustained p95 regression >= +12% and likely real.
- Sev1 (rare): Tier A model, multi-region AND sustained >= +20% OR clear correlation with active rollout and we can stop a fleet-wide impact.

Dampening, anti-flap, and dedupe strategy
Dedupe key (incident-bot and alert manager)
- alert_key = perf_canary:{model}:{model_version}:{region}:{gpu_sku}:{signal_type}
- Additional grouping for incident thread: group by model across regions when multi-region triggers are present, but keep actionable sub-alerts in the thread.

Anti-flap rules
- Require “persistence” (2/3 for warnings, 3/4 for paging)
- Apply hysteresis for recovery:
  - Don’t auto-resolve until metric returns to within (warn_latency_pct / 2) for 2 consecutive windows
- Cooldown windows:
  - WARNING: 30m cooldown per key
  - PAGE: 2h cooldown per key

Baseline drift and baseline freshness
We saw early false positives caused by baseline drift and/or baseline selection mismatches.
Proposed guards:
- “baseline_age_ok”: baseline must be newer than 14 days OR explicitly marked pinned_by_release.
- “baseline_compat_ok”: baseline must match current runtime_build and kernel_tag family (if kernel_tag is being used). If not comparable, downgrade to INFO (no alert) and log “baseline mismatch.”
- If telemetry pipeline latency > 5 minutes (observability health), suppress pages and only post warning with annotation “telemetry_lag=true” (otherwise pages can be late + confusing).

Escalation matrix (who to pull in)
The on-call runbook will contain the detailed decision tree; this is the “who do I ping” table.

1) Likely kernel-level regression
Signals:
- runtime-only latency increases (queueing stable)
- kernel_tag changed recently OR kernel_tag differs from baseline
Escalate to:
- #eng-runtime (primary)
- Direct: Hiro Tanaka (kernel lead) / oncall-runtime
Mitigation owners:
- Runtime team: rollback kernel selection flag, revert kernel change, pin kernel tag

2) Likely scheduler / batching regression
Signals:
- queueing time increases OR batch-size distribution shifts
- runtime-only stable but e2e increases (or vice versa)
Escalate to:
- Sofia Ivanova (scheduling lead) / oncall-runtime
- FYI: Vanessa Ortiz if it coincides with fleet/autoscaling change
Mitigation owners:
- Runtime team: revert scheduler heuristics, adjust batching caps, pin scheduler config
- SRE: if capacity-related, adjust autoscaling / move traffic / drain bad nodes

3) Likely infra / fleet regression
Signals:
- only one region or one GPU SKU
- queueing spikes; correlated with fleet change or host health alerts
Escalate to:
- SRE on-call (primary)
- Fleet/infra partner: Vanessa Ortiz
Mitigation owners:
- SRE: remove bad pool from routing, drain nodes, rollback driver/AMI, shift traffic

4) Likely telemetry/baseline issue (don’t wake people up)
Signals:
- baseline_id missing/mismatch
- sudden label cardinality changes
- metric ingestion lag
Escalate to:
- #eng-platform (Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman)
Mitigation owners:
- Telemetry team: fix pipeline/dashboards/baseline fetch; suppress alert rules while broken

Notification formatting requirements (incident-bot payload)
Each alert message must include, in a compact block:
- Model + version, region, tier, GPU SKU
- Signal type (p95 latency / tps)
- Baseline id + baseline timestamp
- Observed window (time range), min_samples
- Baseline value vs observed value + percent delta
- Attribution hints:
  - runtime-only delta
  - queueing delta
  - runtime_build + kernel_tag
- Links:
  - Grafana dashboard (pre-filtered)
  - Runbook link
  - (Optional) traces link if we have sampling

Proposed dashboard links format
- grafana://perf-canary?var-model={model}&var-region={region}&var-sku={gpu_sku}&from=now-6h&to=now

Open questions / TODOs
- Region-specific thresholds:
  - Likely yes for some SKUs/regions; but we should avoid exploding config surface area. Proposal: only allow per-region overrides for Tier A.
- Runtime build vs kernel tag comparability:
  - If both change frequently, paging rules might be too sensitive. Might need “release window” suppression and tighter coupling to release gates.
- How to handle controlled rollout windows:
  - During a runtime rollout, do we temporarily lower persistence to catch issues faster, or do we suppress to avoid noise? My bias: keep same persistence but add stronger annotation “in_rollout=true” and auto-mention #eng-releases.
- Long-term: should perf canary pages integrate with automatic rollback?
  - PM asked for rollout gates (pause/rollback criteria). We should only do auto-actions once paging has demonstrated high precision.

Change log (draft)
- 2025-12-12: initial policy skeleton from SRE kickoff
- 2026-01-09: added persistence + cooldown recommendations after early warning noise
- 2026-01-24: added baseline freshness/compat guards + telemetry lag suppression
- 2026-02-03: tightened paging scope to Tier A only; added multi-region confidence rule

- Paging only for “top models” (by customer impact) AND only on sustained regressions that pass minimum sample size and confidence rules.
- Dampen aggressively: require persistence, don’t page on single-window spikes, add cooldowns.
- Every alert must include: baseline ref (versioned), observed window stats, attribution hints (queueing vs runtime-only, kernel tag, runtime build, GPU SKU), dashboard + runbook links.

Definitions and assumptions
- Canary sends synthetic requests with fixed workload profiles (prompt length buckets + prefill/decode mix). No customer data.
- Baselines are stored per {model_id, model_version, runtime_build?, kernel_tag?, gpu_sku, region} with explicit versioning. For alerting we pick the “active baseline” for the canary config.
- Regressions we care about (primary):
  - p95 end-to-end latency up
  - tokens/sec down
- Secondary signals for attribution (not paging on these alone initially):
  - runtime-only p95 latency
  - scheduler queueing time p95
  - prefill vs decode breakdown
  - batch size distribution shifts
  - KV cache hit-rate changes (if emitted; not required for MVP)

Metric conventions (names are tentative / for queries)
- perf_canary_request_latency_ms{quantile="0.95",model,model_version,region,tier,gpu_sku,runtime_build,kernel_tag}
- perf_canary_runtime_latency_ms{quantile="0.95",...}
- perf_canary_queueing_latency_ms{quantile="0.95",...}
- perf_canary_tokens_per_second{model,model_version,region,tier,gpu_sku,runtime_build,kernel_tag}
- perf_canary_requests_total{model,...,status="ok|err"}
- perf_canary_baseline_id{model,...} (or exposed via annotation field in alert payload)

Alert classes
1) WARNING (non-paging) — “Heads up, investigate during business hours; validate if real and if tied to a rollout.”
2) PAGE (Sev2 default; Sev1 only if broad/top-tier + persistent + clear user impact risk)

Note: We should avoid “Sev1” for perf-only until we have more confidence in signal fidelity and a clean mitigation path.

Routing targets (current)
- WARNING:
  - Slack: #eng-runtime
  - FYI tag: #eng-platform (if it looks like telemetry/baseline issue)
- PAGE:
  - On-call page: SRE primary (per region rotation)
  - Slack incident thread created by incident-bot in #incidents with auto-mentions:
    - @oncall-sre
    - @oncall-runtime (secondary)
  - If regression correlates to active release window, auto-mention #eng-releases as FYI (not action owner unless we need a freeze).

Model tiers (policy input)
We need an explicit “canary_impact_tier” per model in control plane config.
- Tier A (Top models): eligible for paging once tuned; must be stable in warning for ~1 week.
- Tier B (Important): warning-only unless we see repeated true positives.
- Tier C (Coverage / high-risk changes): warning-only; primarily for release confidence.

Decision notes: page vs warn
- Why not page for all models?
  - Noise risk + on-call load + not all regressions are user-visible (e.g., canary profile mismatch or isolated SKU).
- What do we page on?
  - Only Tier A models OR regressions that strongly suggest imminent user impact (e.g., runtime-only latency up across multiple regions/SKUs).
- Tokens/sec drop vs p95 latency up:
  - Tokens/sec is useful but can be more sensitive to batching policy shifts and decode length distribution. For now, tokens/sec pages require higher confidence/persistence than latency.
- Queueing time:
  - Queueing changes can indicate capacity/fleet issues. However, queueing can also move due to autoscaling churn. We treat queueing as an attribution hint and a separate warning, not a standalone page trigger initially.

Core alert conditions (proposed)
All alerts require:
- min_samples: perf_canary_requests_total{status="ok"} >= N in the evaluation window
- error_rate guardrail: perf_canary_requests_total{status="err"} / total <= 1% (otherwise route to availability/error alert policy; different runbook)
- baseline available and “baseline_age_ok” (see baseline drift section)

Recommended minimum sample sizes (initial)
- For each (model, region, gpu_sku, tier):
  - N_warn = 120 requests per 10m window (or equivalent) for p95 stability
  - N_page = 300 requests aggregated over longer windows (see below)
Notes:
- We can lower N for very expensive models if we extend window length, but avoid windows > 60m for first pass (too slow to react).

WARNING triggers (Tier A/B/C)
A) p95 end-to-end latency regression (warning)
- Condition: p95_e2e_ms >= baseline_p95_e2e_ms * (1 + warn_latency_pct)
- Suggested warn_latency_pct:
  - Tier A: +8%
  - Tier B: +10%
  - Tier C: +12%
- Windowing: evaluate 10m windows, require 2 of last 3 windows to exceed threshold.
- Additional annotation fields:
  - runtime_only_delta_pct
  - queueing_delta_pct
  - kernel_tag, runtime_build, gpu_sku

B) tokens/sec regression (warning)
- Condition: tps <= baseline_tps * (1 - warn_tps_pct)
- Suggested warn_tps_pct:
  - Tier A: -8%
  - Tier B: -10%
  - Tier C: -12%
- Windowing: 10m windows, require 2 of last 3 windows.

PAGE triggers (Tier A only; gated rollout)
Pre-reqs to enable paging for a model:
- At least 7 days in warning-only with <2 false positives/week
- Baseline stable and updated at least once post-deploy per {region, gpu_sku} we page on
- Runbook validated in on-call training (tabletop)

P1) Sustained p95 latency regression (page)
- Condition: p95_e2e_ms >= baseline_p95_e2e_ms * (1 + page_latency_pct)
- Suggested page_latency_pct:
  - Tier A: +12% sustained
- Persistence / dampening:
  - Evaluate 15m windows; require 3 of last 4 windows over threshold (i.e., ~45-60m sustained)
  - Cooldown: once paged, don’t page again for same (model, region, sku) for 2 hours unless severity increases by +10% relative to last page
- Multi-dimension logic:
  - If regression is present across >=2 regions OR across >=2 GPU SKUs in same region, escalate confidence and shorten persistence requirement to 2 of last 3 windows.

P2) Sustained tokens/sec regression (page) — optional / later
- Condition: tps <= baseline_tps * (1 - page_tps_pct)
- Suggested page_tps_pct:
  - Tier A: -15% sustained
- Same persistence/cooldown as P1, but only if p95 latency is also degraded OR runtime-only latency increased.
Rationale: isolated tps drops without latency impact have historically been less actionable for SRE on short timescales.

Severity mapping (PAGE)
- Sev2 (default): single region OR single SKU; sustained p95 regression >= +12% and likely real.
- Sev1 (rare): Tier A model, multi-region AND sustained >= +20% OR clear correlation with active rollout and we can stop a fleet-wide impact.

Dampening, anti-flap, and dedupe strategy
Dedupe key (incident-bot and alert manager)
- alert_key = perf_canary:{model}:{model_version}:{region}:{gpu_sku}:{signal_type}
- Additional grouping for incident thread: group by model across regions when multi-region triggers are present, but keep actionable sub-alerts in the thread.

Anti-flap rules
- Require “persistence” (2/3 for warnings, 3/4 for paging)
- Apply hysteresis for recovery:
  - Don’t auto-resolve until metric returns to within (warn_latency_pct / 2) for 2 consecutive windows
- Cooldown windows:
  - WARNING: 30m cooldown per key
  - PAGE: 2h cooldown per key

Baseline drift and baseline freshness
We saw early false positives caused by baseline drift and/or baseline selection mismatches.
Proposed guards:
- “baseline_age_ok”: baseline must be newer than 14 days OR explicitly marked pinned_by_release.
- “baseline_compat_ok”: baseline must match current runtime_build and kernel_tag family (if kernel_tag is being used). If not comparable, downgrade to INFO (no alert) and log “baseline mismatch.”
- If telemetry pipeline latency > 5 minutes (observability health), suppress pages and only post warning with annotation “telemetry_lag=true” (otherwise pages can be late + confusing).

Escalation matrix (who to pull in)
The on-call runbook will contain the detailed decision tree; this is the “who do I ping” table.

1) Likely kernel-level regression
Signals:
- runtime-only latency increases (queueing stable)
- kernel_tag changed recently OR kernel_tag differs from baseline
Escalate to:
- #eng-runtime (primary)
- Direct: Hiro Tanaka (kernel lead) / oncall-runtime
Mitigation owners:
- Runtime team: rollback kernel selection flag, revert kernel change, pin kernel tag

2) Likely scheduler / batching regression
Signals:
- queueing time increases OR batch-size distribution shifts
- runtime-only stable but e2e increases (or vice versa)
Escalate to:
- Sofia Ivanova (scheduling lead) / oncall-runtime
- FYI: Vanessa Ortiz if it coincides with fleet/autoscaling change
Mitigation owners:
- Runtime team: revert scheduler heuristics, adjust batching caps, pin scheduler config
- SRE: if capacity-related, adjust autoscaling / move traffic / drain bad nodes

3) Likely infra / fleet regression
Signals:
- only one region or one GPU SKU
- queueing spikes; correlated with fleet change or host health alerts
Escalate to:
- SRE on-call (primary)
- Fleet/infra partner: Vanessa Ortiz
Mitigation owners:
- SRE: remove bad pool from routing, drain nodes, rollback driver/AMI, shift traffic

4) Likely telemetry/baseline issue (don’t wake people up)
Signals:
- baseline_id missing/mismatch
- sudden label cardinality changes
- metric ingestion lag
Escalate to:
- #eng-platform (Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman)
Mitigation owners:
- Telemetry team: fix pipeline/dashboards/baseline fetch; suppress alert rules while broken

Notification formatting requirements (incident-bot payload)
Each alert message must include, in a compact block:
- Model + version, region, tier, GPU SKU
- Signal type (p95 latency / tps)
- Baseline id + baseline timestamp
- Observed window (time range), min_samples
- Baseline value vs observed value + percent delta
- Attribution hints:
  - runtime-only delta
  - queueing delta
  - runtime_build + kernel_tag
- Links:
  - Grafana dashboard (pre-filtered)
  - Runbook link
  - (Optional) traces link if we have sampling

Proposed dashboard links format
- grafana://perf-canary?var-model={model}&var-region={region}&var-sku={gpu_sku}&from=now-6h&to=now

Open questions / TODOs
- Region-specific thresholds:
  - Likely yes for some SKUs/regions; but we should avoid exploding config surface area. Proposal: only allow per-region overrides for Tier A.
- Runtime build vs kernel tag comparability:
  - If both change frequently, paging rules might be too sensitive. Might need “release window” suppression and tighter coupling to release gates.
- How to handle controlled rollout windows:
  - During a runtime rollout, do we temporarily lower persistence to catch issues faster, or do we suppress to avoid noise? My bias: keep same persistence but add stronger annotation “in_rollout=true” and auto-mention #eng-releases.
- Long-term: should perf canary pages integrate with automatic rollback?
  - PM asked for rollout gates (pause/rollback criteria). We should only do auto-actions once paging has demonstrated high precision.

Change log (draft)
- 2025-12-12: initial policy skeleton from SRE kickoff
- 2026-01-09: added persistence + cooldown recommendations after early warning noise
- 2026-01-24: added baseline freshness/compat guards + telemetry lag suppression
- 2026-02-03: tightened paging scope to Tier A only; added multi-region confidence rule

- This is intentionally a “policy draft” (not a formal Confluence page yet). Expect edits as we learn from phase-1 rollout.

- Paging only for “top models” (by customer impact) AND only on sustained regressions that pass minimum sample size and confidence rules.

B) tokens/sec regression (warning)

- Persistence / dampening:

  - Cooldown: once paged, don’t page again for same (model, region, sku) for 2 hours unless severity increases by +10% relative to last page

Dampening, anti-flap, and dedupe strategy

- alert_key = perf_canary:{model}:{model_version}:{region}:{gpu_sku}:{signal_type}
- Additional grouping for incident thread: group by model across regions when multi-region triggers are present, but keep actionable sub-alerts in the thread.

  - WARNING: 30m cooldown per key
  - PAGE: 2h cooldown per key

Baseline drift and baseline freshness
We saw early false positives caused by baseline drift and/or baseline selection mismatches.
Proposed guards:
- “baseline_age_ok”: baseline must be newer than 14 days OR explicitly marked pinned_by_release.
- “baseline_compat_ok”: baseline must match current runtime_build and kernel_tag family (if kernel_tag is being used). If not comparable, downgrade to INFO (no alert) and log “baseline mismatch.”

Escalation matrix (who to pull in)
The on-call runbook will contain the detailed decision tree; this is the “who do I ping” table.

1) Likely kernel-level regression
Signals:
- runtime-only latency increases (queueing stable)
- kernel_tag changed recently OR kernel_tag differs from baseline
Escalate to:
- #eng-runtime (primary)
- Direct: Hiro Tanaka (kernel lead) / oncall-runtime
Mitigation owners:
- Runtime team: rollback kernel selection flag, revert kernel change, pin kernel tag

2) Likely scheduler / batching regression
Signals:
- queueing time increases OR batch-size distribution shifts

Each alert message must include, in a compact block:
- Model + version, region, tier, GPU SKU
