source.title: Hot-route capacity protection — MVP scope (WIP)
source.body:
Doc purpose
- Working scope doc for the Hot-route capacity protection MVP.
- Intended readers: SRE + Platform + Routing + Gateway + Runtime.
- This is the “what are we actually shipping first” doc. It is intentionally opinionated; expect edits.

Background / why now
- Motivating incident: 2025-11-03 Sev1 fleet-wide latency cascade triggered by overload on a small set of hot routes (chat + embeddings). Root causes included: no tier-aware admission control, noisy-neighbor amplification in shared pools, and insufficient/late capacity signals.
- Goal: keep the fleet healthy under pressure by protecting enterprise SLOs and shedding best-effort earlier and predictably.

MVP definition (what we will ship)

1) Protected route set (MVP)
- Route group: chat
  - /v1/chat/completions (streaming + non-streaming)
- Route group: embeddings
  - /v1/embeddings
- Route group: rerank
  - /v1/rerank
Notes:
- MVP focuses on the above because they are high-traffic, high-SLA, and have distinct traffic shapes that cause queue growth (long prompts + high concurrency).
- We will allow “protected” designation to be config-driven, but MVP only commits to the above three route groups.

2) Priority classes (MVP)
- Enterprise (protected)
  - Traffic from enterprise-tier accounts and/or contractually protected routes.
  - Target behavior: maintain defined latency/availability SLOs during regional/fleet pressure.
  - Overload behavior: admission control is allowed but should be the last resort; if we shed, do so with explicit reason codes and supportable artifacts.

- Best-effort (default)
  - Self-serve / free / non-protected traffic.
  - Target behavior: stable, predictable throttling; earlier shedding to preserve fleet health and enterprise budgets.
  - Overload behavior: throttle/shed early and clearly.

Notes / nuance:
- We are explicitly *not* introducing many sub-tiers in MVP (e.g., “gold/silver/bronze”) to avoid policy explosion. Keep it to two priority classes in data plane.

3) Enforcement + control loop (MVP behavior)
- Tier-aware rate limiting at the API gateway
  - Enforce account+route-group limits using low-cardinality keys.
  - Consistent 429 response schema + headers.
  - This is the first-line limiter and should cover clear abuse/misconfiguration.

- Priority routing + admission control under pressure
  - When fleet capacity signals indicate pressure (see signals section), routing should preferentially admit Enterprise and throttle/shed Best-effort.
  - The system must avoid “everything slows down” scenarios by proactively reducing queue growth.

- Controlled shedding semantics
  - Responses must be deterministic and explainable (quota exceeded vs overload protection vs policy denied).
  - For streaming clients, termination semantics must be consistent (see open questions and known issues).

4) Policy evaluation contract (MVP)
- A request should be classifiable into:
  - route_group (chat|embeddings|rerank)
  - customer_tier (enterprise|best_effort)
  - model family (optional in MVP; avoid high-cardinality)
  - region
- Policy decisions returned to gateway/routing must include:
  - allow | throttle | shed
  - reason_code (see below)
  - retry_after_ms (if applicable)
  - decision_id / trace handle for internal debugging (non-customer-visible)

5) Standard 429 semantics (MVP)
- MVP will standardize response payload + headers for 429 across gateway and routing.
- Reason codes (initial list; final list owned by Gateway + Support readiness):
  - quota_exceeded (contracted quota window)
  - rate_limited (configured RPS/concurrency limit)
  - overload_protection (capacity protection shed)
  - policy_denied (e.g., route not enabled, tier mismatch)
- Required headers:
  - x-redwood-retry-after-ms: <int>
  - x-redwood-throttle-reason: <reason_code>
  - x-redwood-priority-class: enterprise|best_effort
  - x-redwood-route-group: chat|embeddings|rerank
- Payload requirements:
  - Must be stable for clients parsing programmatically.
  - Must not leak internal capacity numbers.

6) Observability (MVP)
- Metrics must support answering these questions in <5 minutes during an event:
  - Are enterprise routes meeting SLO? If not, what is the dominant failure mode (queue depth, 429 shed, 5xx, timeouts)?
  - Is best-effort shedding occurring as expected (earlier, predictable), and is it preventing 5xx/latency cascade?
  - Is there a single route group / tenant driving pressure?

- MVP dashboard requirements (by route_group + priority_class; keep cardinality bounded):
  - Request rate (RPS) admitted vs attempted
  - 429 rate and breakdown by reason_code
  - Shed rate (% of attempted)
  - p50/p95/p99 latency (server-side)
  - 5xx rate
  - Queue depth + scheduler lag (runtime exported)
  - “Protection active” annotations (feature flag + threshold triggers)

- MVP alerting principles:
  - Page on enterprise SLO violation / burn (not on expected best-effort shedding).
  - Notify (non-page) when overload protection activates above threshold for sustained period.

7) Rollback / safety (MVP)
- Everything behind feature flags.
- Allowlist-based canary (accounts + routes + region).
- One-command rollback to previous enforcement path.
- Safe defaults:
  - If policy service is unavailable: fail-open for Enterprise, fail-closed (rate limit) for Best-effort *only if* it prevents fleet harm. (See open question #7; needs final decision.)


Explicit non-goals (NOT in MVP)
- Billing/pricing changes
  - No dynamic pricing or per-token price changes in MVP.
  - Only add metadata hooks for future billing attribution if low risk.

- Full fairness scheduler rewrite
  - We will not redesign the multi-tenant scheduler.
  - We will only export/add the minimal signals required for admission decisions.

- Customer-by-customer bespoke shaping as a primary mechanism
  - Exceptions will exist, but MVP goal is a sane default policy matrix.

- Perfect global capacity optimization
  - We are not building a global optimizer. Routing may support limited fallback/region policies, but MVP keeps this minimal and guarded.

- Public Console UI for customers
  - Internal operator views are OK. A polished customer-facing UI is not required for MVP GA.


Rollout sequencing (proposed)

Phase 0 — Inventory + policy draft (week 1)
- Confirm protected route group list (chat/embeddings/rerank).
- Identify top enterprise accounts for canary allowlist.
- Draft tier-by-route-group policy matrix (limits, windows, burst allowances).

Phase 1 — Shadow mode / metrics only (week 2)
- Enable classification (route_group + priority_class) end-to-end.
- Emit metrics and logs for “would have throttled/shed” without enforcing (where possible).
- Validate cardinality + dashboard usefulness.

Phase 2 — Gateway rate limits (week 3)
- Turn on tier-aware gateway enforcement for a single region (us-east) for best-effort only.
- Validate 429 semantics and client retry guidance with Support.

Phase 3 — Admission control + priority routing (week 4-5)
- Enable routing admission control using capacity signals.
- Enforce: best-effort shed before enterprise.
- Add incident-bot notifications for protection activation.

Phase 4 — Enterprise canary (week 6)
- Allowlist 3–5 enterprise accounts.
- Expand to all enterprise accounts on protected routes if metrics are stable.
- Gate expansion on SLO + customer comms readiness.

Phase 5 — Multi-region expansion (week 7-8)
- Roll to eu-west after us-east success.
- Validate region-specific thresholds (capacity signals differ).

Phase 6 — GA + cleanup (week 9-10)
- Declare GA when:
  - enterprise routes meet SLO during at least one simulated overload + one real pressure event (or equivalent load test)
  - best-effort shedding is predictable and does not cause pager storms
  - Support playbook + docs shipped
- Remove legacy throttling path to reduce operator confusion (post-GA hardening).


MVP success criteria (SRE-owned)
- During simulated overload on a protected route group:
  - Enterprise: p95 latency stays within agreed SLO bounds (per route group), and 5xx does not materially increase.
  - Best-effort: sees increased 429s (expected), but the fleet avoids tail-latency cascade and 5xx spikes.

- During real incidents/pressure:
  - “Protection active” is visible (dashboards + incident-bot message).
  - On-call can answer “why are customers getting 429s” with reason codes and a runbook.

- Operational overhead:
  - No high-cardinality regressions introduced by new metrics/tags.
  - Policy evaluation overhead does not add meaningful latency to the request path (target: negligible vs network variance; exact budget owned by Platform).


Dependencies / workstreams (rough)
- Gateway: tier-aware rate limiter + consistent 429 schema (Amara + Cole)
- Policy engine: priority classes + deterministic decision logging (Jordan)
- Routing: admission control using capacity signals; preserve enterprise budgets (Mina + Elena)
- Runtime: export queue depth + scheduler lag; keep metric cardinality bounded (Sofia + Julian)
- Observability: dashboards + burn alerts + annotations; incident-bot integration (Allison + Sergio + Logan)
- Program mgmt: canary list, rollout windows, go/no-go checklists (Talia)


Open questions (needs answers before GA)
1) What is the budget primitive for “route protection”?
   - Options: RPS, concurrent requests, tokens/sec admitted, GPU-seconds, or a hybrid.
   - SRE preference: use a simple gating signal in MVP (queue depth + scheduler lag) + enforce coarse limits (RPS/concurrency) at gateway. Revisit token-based once metering is stable.

2) How do we define enterprise SLO targets per route group for MVP?
   - Need explicit targets for chat vs embeddings vs rerank (latency + availability).
   - Owner: SRE + Product (Hosted API). Must align with existing enterprise commitments.

3) Streaming semantics during shedding
   - If a request is admitted but later shed mid-stream, what does the client see?
   - MVP ask: consistent termination + final message chunk with reason_code (if possible) OR immediate termination with standardized error event.
   - Needs alignment with Gateway + Docs.

4) Fallback policy in MVP: allowed or not?
   - For enterprise protected routes, do we ever fallback to alternate model/region to preserve availability?
   - Risk: silent quality changes / compliance constraints.
   - Proposed MVP stance: default is “no fallback” for enterprise; allow explicit per-account override with approvals. Best-effort can fallback if safe.

5) Dedicated tier interaction
   - If Dedicated customers share none of the hosted pools, do they need this MVP?
   - Current assumption: Dedicated still benefits from tiered admission within a cluster; MVP focuses on Hosted API first, but keep config plumbing reusable.

6) Failure mode behavior if policy/routing dependency is degraded
   - If policy engine times out: do we fail-open or fail-closed?
   - Proposed: fail-open for Enterprise on protected routes (favor availability) but with a circuit breaker to prevent runaway; fail-closed for Best-effort (return 429 rate_limited) to protect fleet.
   - Needs explicit ADR + security review due to abuse concerns.

7) Cardinality guardrails
   - Which labels/tags are allowed for overload metrics?
   - Proposed allowed dimensions: route_group, priority_class, region, outcome, reason_code.
   - Explicitly disallow: tenant_id, api_key_id, raw model_id, prompt length bucket finer than coarse buckets.

8) Exceptions process (support + platform)
   - What’s the approval flow for a temporary enterprise burst exception?
   - Proposed: Support escalates -> Solutions Eng qualifies -> SRE/Platform approves with expiration + monitoring gate.
   - Need a standard “exception record” location (ticket + config PR + expiry reminder).

9) Load test / game day definition
   - What is the minimum credible simulation that counts for GA?
   - Proposed: one controlled overload per route_group in us-east demonstrating enterprise SLO preservation while best-effort sheds.


Notes / scratchpad (keep / remove later)
- The hardest part operationally is not “do we shed” but “can we explain it.” Reason codes + dashboards + deterministic logs are non-negotiable.
- We should be careful that best-effort shedding does not look like random flakiness; 429s must be consistent and retries must work.
- If we can’t get token-based budgeting correct in MVP, do not block; ship with coarse controls + clear signals.

Changelog
- 2025-12-10: initial draft created after SRE+Platform scoping meeting.
- 2026-01-07: added rollout sequencing + draft reason codes.
- 2026-01-29: updated open questions (fallback stance, policy engine failure mode), aligned dependency owners.

Doc purpose
- Working scope doc for the Hot-route capacity protection MVP.
- Intended readers: SRE + Platform + Routing + Gateway + Runtime.
- This is the “what are we actually shipping first” doc. It is intentionally opinionated; expect edits.

Background / why now
- Motivating incident: 2025-11-03 Sev1 fleet-wide latency cascade triggered by overload on a small set of hot routes (chat + embeddings). Root causes included: no tier-aware admission control, noisy-neighbor amplification in shared pools, and insufficient/late capacity signals.
- Goal: keep the fleet healthy under pressure by protecting enterprise SLOs and shedding best-effort earlier and predictably.

MVP definition (what we will ship)

1) Protected route set (MVP)
- Route group: chat
  - /v1/chat/completions (streaming + non-streaming)
- Route group: embeddings
  - /v1/embeddings
- Route group: rerank
  - /v1/rerank
Notes:
- MVP focuses on the above because they are high-traffic, high-SLA, and have distinct traffic shapes that cause queue growth (long prompts + high concurrency).
- We will allow “protected” designation to be config-driven, but MVP only commits to the above three route groups.

2) Priority classes (MVP)
- Enterprise (protected)
  - Traffic from enterprise-tier accounts and/or contractually protected routes.
  - Target behavior: maintain defined latency/availability SLOs during regional/fleet pressure.
  - Overload behavior: admission control is allowed but should be the last resort; if we shed, do so with explicit reason codes and supportable artifacts.

- Best-effort (default)
  - Self-serve / free / non-protected traffic.
  - Target behavior: stable, predictable throttling; earlier shedding to preserve fleet health and enterprise budgets.
  - Overload behavior: throttle/shed early and clearly.

Notes / nuance:
- We are explicitly *not* introducing many sub-tiers in MVP (e.g., “gold/silver/bronze”) to avoid policy explosion. Keep it to two priority classes in data plane.

3) Enforcement + control loop (MVP behavior)
- Tier-aware rate limiting at the API gateway
  - Enforce account+route-group limits using low-cardinality keys.
  - Consistent 429 response schema + headers.
  - This is the first-line limiter and should cover clear abuse/misconfiguration.

- Priority routing + admission control under pressure
  - When fleet capacity signals indicate pressure (see signals section), routing should preferentially admit Enterprise and throttle/shed Best-effort.
  - The system must avoid “everything slows down” scenarios by proactively reducing queue growth.

- Controlled shedding semantics
  - Responses must be deterministic and explainable (quota exceeded vs overload protection vs policy denied).
  - For streaming clients, termination semantics must be consistent (see open questions and known issues).

4) Policy evaluation contract (MVP)
- A request should be classifiable into:
  - route_group (chat|embeddings|rerank)
  - customer_tier (enterprise|best_effort)
  - model family (optional in MVP; avoid high-cardinality)
  - region
- Policy decisions returned to gateway/routing must include:
  - allow | throttle | shed
  - reason_code (see below)
  - retry_after_ms (if applicable)
  - decision_id / trace handle for internal debugging (non-customer-visible)

5) Standard 429 semantics (MVP)
- MVP will standardize response payload + headers for 429 across gateway and routing.
- Reason codes (initial list; final list owned by Gateway + Support readiness):
  - quota_exceeded (contracted quota window)
  - rate_limited (configured RPS/concurrency limit)
  - overload_protection (capacity protection shed)
  - policy_denied (e.g., route not enabled, tier mismatch)
- Required headers:
  - x-redwood-retry-after-ms: <int>
  - x-redwood-throttle-reason: <reason_code>
  - x-redwood-priority-class: enterprise|best_effort
  - x-redwood-route-group: chat|embeddings|rerank
- Payload requirements:
  - Must be stable for clients parsing programmatically.
  - Must not leak internal capacity numbers.

6) Observability (MVP)
- Metrics must support answering these questions in <5 minutes during an event:
  - Are enterprise routes meeting SLO? If not, what is the dominant failure mode (queue depth, 429 shed, 5xx, timeouts)?
  - Is best-effort shedding occurring as expected (earlier, predictable), and is it preventing 5xx/latency cascade?
  - Is there a single route group / tenant driving pressure?

- MVP dashboard requirements (by route_group + priority_class; keep cardinality bounded):
  - Request rate (RPS) admitted vs attempted
  - 429 rate and breakdown by reason_code
  - Shed rate (% of attempted)
  - p50/p95/p99 latency (server-side)
  - 5xx rate
  - Queue depth + scheduler lag (runtime exported)
  - “Protection active” annotations (feature flag + threshold triggers)

- MVP alerting principles:
  - Page on enterprise SLO violation / burn (not on expected best-effort shedding).
  - Notify (non-page) when overload protection activates above threshold for sustained period.

7) Rollback / safety (MVP)
- Everything behind feature flags.
- Allowlist-based canary (accounts + routes + region).
- One-command rollback to previous enforcement path.
- Safe defaults:
  - If policy service is unavailable: fail-open for Enterprise, fail-closed (rate limit) for Best-effort *only if* it prevents fleet harm. (See open question #7; needs final decision.)


Explicit non-goals (NOT in MVP)
- Billing/pricing changes
  - No dynamic pricing or per-token price changes in MVP.
  - Only add metadata hooks for future billing attribution if low risk.

- Full fairness scheduler rewrite
  - We will not redesign the multi-tenant scheduler.
  - We will only export/add the minimal signals required for admission decisions.

- Customer-by-customer bespoke shaping as a primary mechanism
  - Exceptions will exist, but MVP goal is a sane default policy matrix.

- Perfect global capacity optimization
  - We are not building a global optimizer. Routing may support limited fallback/region policies, but MVP keeps this minimal and guarded.

- Public Console UI for customers
  - Internal operator views are OK. A polished customer-facing UI is not required for MVP GA.


Rollout sequencing (proposed)

Phase 0 — Inventory + policy draft (week 1)
- Confirm protected route group list (chat/embeddings/rerank).
- Identify top enterprise accounts for canary allowlist.
- Draft tier-by-route-group policy matrix (limits, windows, burst allowances).

Phase 1 — Shadow mode / metrics only (week 2)
- Enable classification (route_group + priority_class) end-to-end.
- Emit metrics and logs for “would have throttled/shed” without enforcing (where possible).
- Validate cardinality + dashboard usefulness.

Phase 2 — Gateway rate limits (week 3)
- Turn on tier-aware gateway enforcement for a single region (us-east) for best-effort only.
- Validate 429 semantics and client retry guidance with Support.

Phase 3 — Admission control + priority routing (week 4-5)
- Enable routing admission control using capacity signals.
- Enforce: best-effort shed before enterprise.
- Add incident-bot notifications for protection activation.

Phase 4 — Enterprise canary (week 6)
- Allowlist 3–5 enterprise accounts.
- Expand to all enterprise accounts on protected routes if metrics are stable.
- Gate expansion on SLO + customer comms readiness.

Phase 5 — Multi-region expansion (week 7-8)
- Roll to eu-west after us-east success.
- Validate region-specific thresholds (capacity signals differ).

Phase 6 — GA + cleanup (week 9-10)
- Declare GA when:
  - enterprise routes meet SLO during at least one simulated overload + one real pressure event (or equivalent load test)
  - best-effort shedding is predictable and does not cause pager storms
  - Support playbook + docs shipped
- Remove legacy throttling path to reduce operator confusion (post-GA hardening).


MVP success criteria (SRE-owned)
- During simulated overload on a protected route group:
  - Enterprise: p95 latency stays within agreed SLO bounds (per route group), and 5xx does not materially increase.
  - Best-effort: sees increased 429s (expected), but the fleet avoids tail-latency cascade and 5xx spikes.

- During real incidents/pressure:
  - “Protection active” is visible (dashboards + incident-bot message).
  - On-call can answer “why are customers getting 429s” with reason codes and a runbook.

- Operational overhead:
  - No high-cardinality regressions introduced by new metrics/tags.
  - Policy evaluation overhead does not add meaningful latency to the request path (target: negligible vs network variance; exact budget owned by Platform).


Dependencies / workstreams (rough)
- Gateway: tier-aware rate limiter + consistent 429 schema (Amara + Cole)
- Policy engine: priority classes + deterministic decision logging (Jordan)
- Routing: admission control using capacity signals; preserve enterprise budgets (Mina + Elena)
- Runtime: export queue depth + scheduler lag; keep metric cardinality bounded (Sofia + Julian)
- Observability: dashboards + burn alerts + annotations; incident-bot integration (Allison + Sergio + Logan)
- Program mgmt: canary list, rollout windows, go/no-go checklists (Talia)


Open questions (needs answers before GA)
1) What is the budget primitive for “route protection”?
   - Options: RPS, concurrent requests, tokens/sec admitted, GPU-seconds, or a hybrid.
   - SRE preference: use a simple gating signal in MVP (queue depth + scheduler lag) + enforce coarse limits (RPS/concurrency) at gateway. Revisit token-based once metering is stable.

2) How do we define enterprise SLO targets per route group for MVP?
   - Need explicit targets for chat vs embeddings vs rerank (latency + availability).
   - Owner: SRE + Product (Hosted API). Must align with existing enterprise commitments.

3) Streaming semantics during shedding
   - If a request is admitted but later shed mid-stream, what does the client see?
   - MVP ask: consistent termination + final message chunk with reason_code (if possible) OR immediate termination with standardized error event.
   - Needs alignment with Gateway + Docs.

4) Fallback policy in MVP: allowed or not?
   - For enterprise protected routes, do we ever fallback to alternate model/region to preserve availability?
   - Risk: silent quality changes / compliance constraints.
   - Proposed MVP stance: default is “no fallback” for enterprise; allow explicit per-account override with approvals. Best-effort can fallback if safe.

5) Dedicated tier interaction
   - If Dedicated customers share none of the hosted pools, do they need this MVP?
   - Current assumption: Dedicated still benefits from tiered admission within a cluster; MVP focuses on Hosted API first, but keep config plumbing reusable.

6) Failure mode behavior if policy/routing dependency is degraded
   - If policy engine times out: do we fail-open or fail-closed?
   - Proposed: fail-open for Enterprise on protected routes (favor availability) but with a circuit breaker to prevent runaway; fail-closed for Best-effort (return 429 rate_limited) to protect fleet.
   - Needs explicit ADR + security review due to abuse concerns.

7) Cardinality guardrails
   - Which labels/tags are allowed for overload metrics?
   - Proposed allowed dimensions: route_group, priority_class, region, outcome, reason_code.
   - Explicitly disallow: tenant_id, api_key_id, raw model_id, prompt length bucket finer than coarse buckets.

8) Exceptions process (support + platform)
   - What’s the approval flow for a temporary enterprise burst exception?
   - Proposed: Support escalates -> Solutions Eng qualifies -> SRE/Platform approves with expiration + monitoring gate.
   - Need a standard “exception record” location (ticket + config PR + expiry reminder).

9) Load test / game day definition
   - What is the minimum credible simulation that counts for GA?
   - Proposed: one controlled overload per route_group in us-east demonstrating enterprise SLO preservation while best-effort sheds.

   - If policy engine times out: do we fail-open or fail-closed?
   - Proposed: fail-open for Enterprise on protected routes (favor availability) but with a circuit breaker to prevent runaway; fail-closed for Best-effort (return 429 rate_limited) to protect fleet.
   - Needs explicit ADR + security review due to abuse concerns.

7) Cardinality guardrails
   - Which labels/tags are allowed for overload metrics?
   - Proposed allowed dimensions: route_group, priority_class, region, outcome, reason_code.
   - Explicitly disallow: tenant_id, api_key_id, raw model_id, prompt length bucket finer than coarse buckets.

8) Exceptions process (support + platform)
   - What’s the approval flow for a temporary enterprise burst exception?
   - Proposed: Support escalates -> Solutions Eng qualifies -> SRE/Platform approves with expiration + monitoring gate.
   - Need a standard “exception record” location (ticket + config PR + expiry reminder).

9) Load test / game day definition
   - What is the minimum credible simulation that counts for GA?
   - Proposed: one controlled overload per route_group in us-east demonstrating enterprise SLO preservation while best-effort sheds.


Notes / scratchpad (keep / remove later)
- The hardest part operationally is not “do we shed” but “can we explain it.” Reason codes + dashboards + deterministic logs are non-negotiable.
- We should be careful that best-effort shedding does not look like random flakiness; 429s must be consistent and retries must work.
- If we can’t get token-based budgeting correct in MVP, do not block; ship with coarse controls + clear signals.

Changelog
- 2025-12-10: initial draft created after SRE+Platform scoping meeting.
- 2026-01-07: added rollout sequencing + draft reason codes.
- 2026-01-29: updated open questions (fallback stance, policy engine failure mode), aligned dependency owners.

source.title: Customer comms draft — planned regional failover test (Hosted API + Dedicated)
source.content:
Audience: internal draft for SRE + Support/CS + PM review
Owner: SRE (Rafael) with Support liaison (Dev/Owen)

Purpose
We are planning a controlled regional failover test as part of the Reliability Program (regional failover automation upgrade). This doc contains draft customer-facing communications: who we notify, what we promise (and what we do NOT promise), timing, and FAQs/macros for Support.

High-level intent (customer-safe framing)
- We are performing a planned resilience test of our regional traffic steering/failover systems.
- Goal is to improve reliability and reduce impact during real regional incidents.
- We expect no sustained downtime; however, a small subset of requests may see transient errors/timeouts or brief latency increases during traffic shift.

What this is / is not
This IS:
- A controlled, time-boxed test of regional traffic shifting (Hosted API + limited Dedicated participants).
- Conducted with explicit rollback criteria, on-call coverage, and monitoring.

This is NOT:
- A change in customer contract/SLA.
- A promise of zero errors during the test window.
- A “chaos event” without guardrails (we will rollback if error/latency thresholds are exceeded).

Proposed test window (example — adjust per go/no-go)
Primary window:
- Date: Thu 2026-01-15
- Time: 09:00–09:30 PT (17:00–17:30 UTC)
- Expected active traffic shift: ~5–10 minutes inside that window
Fallback window:
- Fri 2026-01-16 09:00–09:30 PT

Scope of customer impact (how we describe it)
Hosted API:
- Potential impact: brief increases in p95/p99 latency, occasional 5xx/timeouts, and (for streaming) possible disconnect requiring reconnect.
- Expected duration: seconds to a few minutes for affected clients; not continuous for the full window.
- Mitigation: routing automation with hold-down timers, client-friendly backpressure (Retry-After where applicable), and rapid rollback.

Dedicated:
- Only pre-selected Dedicated tenants will be included.
- Potential impact: brief elevated queueing/latency during traffic shift; no planned data-plane changes for non-participating tenants.
- We will not include Dedicated tenants with strict data residency constraints unless explicitly approved and configured.

Data residency + security boundary statement (must be consistent)
- We will not route or replicate customer data across regions in a way that violates agreed data residency constraints.
- Customers with region-pinned / residency-restricted configurations will either:
  a) be excluded from the test, OR
  b) participate only within approved regions and with explicit confirmation from their account team.

Who we notify (notification tiers)
Tier 0 — No proactive outreach (status page only)
Criteria:
- Self-serve Hosted API customers not on an enterprise notification list.
Action:
- Post a short status page notice (Planned Maintenance / Resilience Test) 24 hours prior.
- Console banner (if enabled) during the window.

Tier 1 — Proactive notice (email) to enterprise Hosted API accounts
Criteria:
- Enterprise Hosted API accounts with named Support contacts and/or elevated availability requirements.
- Accounts that have previously escalated on reliability, multi-region routing, or auditability.
Action:
- Email to primary + secondary technical contacts 3–5 business days prior.
- Include exact window, expected symptoms, and recommended client behavior.

Tier 2 — Proactive notice (email + CSM confirmation) to participating Dedicated accounts
Criteria:
- Dedicated tenants selected for participation.
- Any tenant where drain/failover procedures could be visible (e.g., traffic shift, brief queueing changes).
Action:
- Email 5–7 business days prior.
- CSM/CS to confirm receipt and validate constraints (residency, peak hours, operational freeze windows).

Explicit exclusions / “do not notify” notes
- Do not include accounts with contractual no-maintenance-window restrictions without Legal/CS sign-off.
- Do not include regulated/residency-constrained tenants unless the failover path is guaranteed compliant and the account team has documented approval.

Approval + ownership (internal)
Draft approval checklist before sending any customer comms:
- SRE (Rafael/Miles): scope + risk statement accurate
- Product (Nikhil/Lauren): customer expectations aligned (Hosted vs Dedicated)
- Support/CS (Dev/Owen): macros + escalation path ready
- Security/Compliance (as needed): residency statement vetted
- Executive go/no-go: Sean Gallagher (SRE leadership) + Neha Kapoor (exec sponsor) per go/no-go criteria thread

Customer email template — Hosted API (Tier 1)
Subject: Planned resilience test — brief traffic shift between regions (date/time)

Hi <Name>,

We’re writing to share a planned resilience test for Redwood Inference’s Hosted API. During this controlled test, we will temporarily shift a portion of traffic between regions to validate our automated failover systems.

When:
- <Day, Date>, <Start–End> <Time zone> (fallback window: <...>)

What you may notice:
- Brief increases in latency, and a small number of timeouts/5xx responses
- If you use streaming responses, a disconnect may occur and your client may need to reconnect

What we’re doing to minimize impact:
- The test is time-boxed and closely monitored by on-call SRE and Routing teams
- We have explicit rollback criteria and will revert quickly if thresholds are exceeded

Recommended client behavior (if applicable):
- Ensure retries are bounded with exponential backoff
- Treat 429/503 with Retry-After as a signal to back off
- For streaming, implement reconnect logic and idempotency where possible

If you have constraints around this window (e.g., peak traffic), reply to this email and we will coordinate.

Thanks,
<Sender name>
Redwood Inference Reliability

Customer email template — Dedicated participant (Tier 2)
Subject: Action requested: confirm window for planned failover resilience test (Dedicated)

Hi <Name>,

As part of our reliability improvements, we’d like to include <Customer/Workspace> in a controlled regional failover test for your Dedicated capacity.

When:
- Proposed: <Day, Date>, <Start–End> <Time zone>

Expected impact:
- We expect your service to remain available.
- You may see brief increases in latency/queueing and a small number of transient errors during the traffic shift.

Safety + controls:
- Test is monitored live with rollback criteria.
- We will not violate any data residency constraints; please confirm your approved regions and any blackout windows.

Please confirm by <date>:
1) Your preferred window (or propose an alternative)
2) Any known peak traffic periods / change freezes
3) Your technical point of contact during the window

Thanks,
<Sender name> + <CSM>

Status page draft (short)
Title: Planned resilience test — regional traffic shift
Body:
We will perform a controlled resilience test that temporarily shifts a portion of traffic between regions to validate automated failover systems.

Window: <Day, Date> <Start–End> <Time zone>
Potential impact: Some customers may experience brief increases in latency and intermittent timeouts/5xx errors. Streaming responses may disconnect and require reconnect.

We will monitor closely and rollback if impact exceeds thresholds. We will post updates during and after the test.

Console banner copy (short)
“Planned resilience test in progress. You may see brief latency increases or transient errors while traffic shifts across regions. We’re monitoring and will rollback if needed.”

Support macro — incoming ticket during window
Hi <Name>,

Thanks for reporting this. We’re currently running a planned resilience test that includes a controlled regional traffic shift. During this window, some clients may see brief timeouts/5xx errors or elevated latency.

What you can do:
- Retry with exponential backoff (respect Retry-After when present)
- If using streaming, reconnect and resume using your idempotency strategy

We’re monitoring live and will rollback if thresholds are exceeded. If you can share request IDs/timestamps and your region preference (if any), we can correlate quickly.

Support macro — after window (resolved)
Hi <Name>,

The planned resilience test has completed and systems are stable. If you’re still seeing issues, please send timestamps/request IDs and we’ll investigate as a separate incident.

FAQ (customer-facing)
Q: Why are you doing this?
A: Controlled tests validate our automated failover systems so that in a real regional incident we can detect issues faster, fail over safely, and reduce customer-visible impact.

Q: Will there be downtime?
A: We do not expect sustained downtime. Some customers may see brief transient errors/timeouts or latency increases during the traffic shift. We will rollback if impact exceeds pre-defined thresholds.

Q: Will my data leave my region?
A: We will not route in a way that violates agreed data residency constraints. If your configuration requires region pinning, you will be excluded from this test or limited to approved regions.

Q: I use streaming. What should I expect?
A: Streaming connections may disconnect during a region redirect. Clients should be prepared to reconnect and continue with an idempotency/resume approach where possible.

Q: Do I need to change anything?
A: No required changes. As a best practice, ensure retries are bounded with exponential backoff, and respect Retry-After on overload responses.

Q: How will you communicate updates?
A: We will post status page updates during the window and notify pre-identified enterprise/Dedicated contacts as applicable.

Q: Can we opt out?
A: Yes. If you have constraints around the window or prefer not to be included (Dedicated), contact your CSM or reply to the notification email.

Timing plan (internal execution)
T-7 to T-5 business days:
- Identify Tier 2 Dedicated participants and confirm constraints with account teams
- Draft email list + send for internal approval

T-3 to T-2 business days:
- Send Tier 1 enterprise Hosted API email
- Send Tier 2 Dedicated email (if not already)

T-24 hours:
- Post status page notice (if using)
- Ensure Support macros are in place and on-call knows where to find them

T-0:
- Start-of-window status page update
- If impact exceeds thresholds: rollback + communicate “test paused/rolled back”

T+30–60 min:
- Post completion update
- Summarize outcomes internally; capture any customer-reported issues

Open questions (need answers before final)
- Final date/time window and time zones for customer comms (confirm go/no-go)
- Which Dedicated accounts are participating (CSM list)
- Whether we will show a Console banner to all users or only impacted tenants
- Final wording for data residency statement (Security/Compliance review)

Notes / do-not-say list (avoid overpromising)
- Avoid “no impact” or “zero downtime” language.
- Avoid specifying exact percentages of errors unless we are comfortable defending them.
- Avoid implying customer SLAs changed.

Change log (brief)
- 2026-01-13: Added streaming-specific guidance + opt-out wording; aligned residency language with security boundaries guidance; added Support macros.

A: Controlled tests validate our automated failover systems so that in a real regional incident we can detect issues faster, fail over safely, and reduce customer-visible impact.

Q: Will there be downtime?
A: We do not expect sustained downtime. Some customers may see brief transient errors/timeouts or latency increases during the traffic shift. We will rollback if impact exceeds pre-defined thresholds.

Q: Will my data leave my region?
A: We will not route in a way that violates agreed data residency constraints. If your configuration requires region pinning, you will be excluded from this test or limited to approved regions.

Q: I use streaming. What should I expect?
A: Streaming connections may disconnect during a region redirect. Clients should be prepared to reconnect and continue with an idempotency/resume approach where possible.

Q: Do I need to change anything?
A: No required changes. As a best practice, ensure retries are bounded with exponential backoff, and respect Retry-After on overload responses.

Q: How will you communicate updates?
A: We will post status page updates during the window and notify pre-identified enterprise/Dedicated contacts as applicable.

Q: Can we opt out?
A: Yes. If you have constraints around the window or prefer not to be included (Dedicated), contact your CSM or reply to the notification email.

Timing plan (internal execution)
T-7 to T-5 business days:
- Identify Tier 2 Dedicated participants and confirm constraints with account teams
- Draft email list + send for internal approval

T-3 to T-2 business days:
- Send Tier 1 enterprise Hosted API email
- Send Tier 2 Dedicated email (if not already)

T-24 hours:
- Post status page notice (if using)
- Ensure Support macros are in place and on-call knows where to find them

T-0:
- Start-of-window status page update
- If impact exceeds thresholds: rollback + communicate “test paused/rolled back”

T+30–60 min:
- Post completion update
- Summarize outcomes internally; capture any customer-reported issues

Open questions (need answers before final)
- Final date/time window and time zones for customer comms (confirm go/no-go)
- Which Dedicated accounts are participating (CSM list)
- Whether we will show a Console banner to all users or only impacted tenants
- Final wording for data residency statement (Security/Compliance review)

Notes / do-not-say list (avoid overpromising)
- Avoid “no impact” or “zero downtime” language.
- Avoid specifying exact percentages of errors unless we are comfortable defending them.
- Avoid implying customer SLAs changed.

Change log (brief)
- 2026-01-13: Added streaming-specific guidance + opt-out wording; aligned residency language with security boundaries guidance; added Support macros.

source.title: Incident type decision tree (draft) — on-call quick classification
source.body:
Purpose
- Provide a fast, consistent incident_type + subtype selection during live incidents.
- Optimize for “good enough in 2–5 minutes” classification that can be refined later.
- Keep v1 low-cardinality: choose exactly 1 primary incident_type/subtype; list other contributing areas separately (postmortem / incident-bot contributing_systems).

Scope reminders (v1)
- In-scope: availability (5xx/overload), latency regressions, streaming disconnects, routing/fallback misbehavior, capacity/fleet shortages, control plane outages, auth/RBAC failures, telemetry/metrics blind spots, private deployment connectivity/config issues.
- Out-of-scope: deep RCA framework (belongs in postmortem), long-term roadmap changes not tied to incident reduction.

How to use this during an incident
1) Identify customer tier(s) impacted: Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private.
2) Confirm the dominant symptom from user perspective (errors vs slow vs disconnect vs cannot authenticate vs cannot change config).
3) Pick the best matching incident_type/subtype below.
4) Set incident_type + subtype in incident-bot ASAP (target: within 15 minutes of incident declaration). Update later if new evidence changes the primary driver.

Incident-bot workflow (current)
- /incident set-type  (select from dropdown; subtype required)
- /incident set-tier  (Hosted/Dedicated/Private/multi)
- /incident set-owner (auto-resolved from type/subtype; verify)
- If you truly cannot decide: set incident_type=Unknown/Needs-triage, add a note, and set a reminder to re-evaluate in 30 minutes.

Rule for multi-cause incidents (important)
- Choose the primary incident_type based on what first restored customer impact when mitigated.
  - Example: capacity shortage caused overload, but a routing policy bug prevented fallback. If fixing routing immediately restored success rate, primary = Routing/Fallback misbehavior; capacity becomes contributing.
  - If adding capacity immediately restored success rate and routing behaved as expected, primary = Capacity/Fleet shortage; routing becomes contributing.

Decision tree (quick checks)

START: Is there confirmed customer impact?
- Yes: proceed.
- Unclear: if only internal alerts without user impact, still classify if Sev0/Sev1 internal reliability incident; otherwise track as degraded signal / investigation.

A) Are we seeing elevated error rates (4xx/5xx) for inference requests?
A1) 5xx spike (or gateway 502/503/504) is the dominant symptom
  -> Go to A1a
A2) 429 spike is the dominant symptom
  -> Go to A2a
A3) Mostly 401/403 authentication/authorization errors
  -> Go to A3a
A4) Mostly client-side errors, but strongly correlated with streaming disconnects (e.g., broken pipe, reset)
  -> Go to C (Streaming)

A1a) 5xx dominant: identify where errors originate
- If errors concentrated in one region/cluster and correlate with “no capacity” / saturation signals
  => incident_type: Capacity/Fleet shortage
     subtypes (pick one):
     - Regional capacity exhaustion (GPU saturation)
     - Provisioning lag / scale-out delay
     - Fleet health degradation (node failures/draining)
     - Quota misconfiguration causing effective capacity drop (if 5xx, not 429)
  Primary dashboards:
  - Grafana: Fleet Capacity Overview (SRE) — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-capacity-overview
  - Grafana: GPU Saturation by Pool — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/gpu-saturation
  Common mitigations:
  - Add/burst capacity; shed load; raise pool limits; move traffic; disable expensive features; coordinate region shift.

- If router fallback rate spikes and “fallback_reason” suggests policy/health misbehavior (not just capacity)
  => incident_type: Routing/Fallback misbehavior
     subtypes:
     - Fallback loop / oscillation
     - Incorrect policy evaluation (routing config)
     - Health signal mismatch (false unhealthy/healthy)
     - Region failover misbehavior (stuck, partial, flapping)
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Routing + Fallback Reasons — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/router-fallback
  - Grafana: Region Failover Health — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/region-failover
  Notes:
  - If cloud provider outage triggers failover, still classify based on our system behavior; add “external_dependency” in notes.

- If 5xx correlates with a recent release/rollout (control plane or runtime) and rollback fixes it
  => incident_type: Rollout/Release regression
     subtypes:
     - Runtime rollout regression
     - Router/control-plane rollout regression
     - Config rollout regression
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Deployments + Error Overlay — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/deploy-overlay
  Mitigations:
  - Roll back/canary halt; pin model/runtime version; revert config.

- If 5xx is caused by control plane request failures (e.g., cannot create/update routes; config write failures) OR widespread config fetch failures
  => incident_type: Control plane outage
     subtypes:
     - API errors (read path)
     - API errors (write path)
     - Config propagation failure
     - Control plane dependency outage (DB/queue)
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Control Plane SLO — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/control-plane-slo
  Notes:
  - If inference serving is fine but customers cannot change config, still treat as customer-impacting (especially Dedicated/Private).

- If 5xx is due to serving runtime failures (OOM, scheduler stall, KV cache thrash) and shows up in runtime metrics/logs
  => incident_type: Serving runtime failure/regression
     subtypes:
     - OOM / memory fragmentation
     - KV cache thrash / eviction storm
     - Scheduler stalls / deadlocks
     - Token generation stalls / timeouts
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Runtime Health (per model) — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/runtime-health
  - Logs: Runtime error signatures — https://logs.redwood.internal/s/runtime-errors

- If you cannot attribute errors because observability is missing/incorrect (no metrics/log gaps)
  => incident_type: Telemetry/Observability blind spot
     subtypes:
     - Metrics ingestion delay/drop
     - Logging pipeline failure
     - Alerting misconfiguration / false positives
     - Dashboard query failure/permission
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Observability Pipeline — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/obs-pipeline

A2a) 429 dominant: throttling vs saturation vs policy
- If 429s align with documented quota/rate limit behavior and recent config change
  => incident_type: Quota/Rate limiting enforcement
     subtypes:
     - Misconfigured quota
     - Unexpected rate limiter behavior
     - Account-level enforcement bug
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Rate Limiter — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/rate-limiter

- If 429s appear during bursts on Dedicated and capacity is saturated (GPU near 100%, queueing high)
  => incident_type: Capacity/Fleet shortage
     subtype: Dedicated pool saturation (burst)
  Note:
  - We still classify as capacity if the system is correctly protecting itself, but add customer tier and “expected protection vs unexpected” in notes.

A3a) 401/403 dominant
=> incident_type: Auth/RBAC failure
   subtypes:
   - Token validation failure
   - RBAC policy evaluation failure
   - Key rotation / KMS integration issue
   - Identity provider dependency outage
Dashboards:
- Grafana: Auth + RBAC Errors — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/auth-rbac
Notes:
- If suspicious pattern (credential stuffing, anomalous geo) loop in Security Ops and dual-track as security incident per policy.

B) If not errors: are we seeing latency regressions (p95/p99) with success rate mostly stable?
B1) Latency elevated across multiple models/routes/regions
  -> Go to B1a
B2) Latency elevated for a specific model version or after model rollout
  -> Go to B2a
B3) Latency elevated but metrics are inconsistent/untrusted
  -> Go to D (Telemetry)

B1a) Broad latency regression
- If queueing time increases and GPU saturation is high
  => incident_type: Capacity/Fleet shortage
     subtype: Saturation-driven latency (queueing)
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Queueing + Concurrency — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/serving-queue

- If router is sending traffic to higher-latency region/model unexpectedly (policy/routing)
  => incident_type: Routing/Fallback misbehavior
     subtype: Policy-induced latency regression
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Route Decision Breakdown — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/route-decisions

- If runtime-level regressions correlate with a build/version change (scheduler/KV cache)
  => incident_type: Serving runtime failure/regression
     subtype: Throughput regression (runtime)

B2a) Latency regression tied to a rollout/config change
=> incident_type: Rollout/Release regression
   subtype: Model/version rollout regression (latency)
Dashboards:
- Grafana: Canary / Perf Overlays — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/perf-canary

C) Are we seeing streaming issues (disconnects, resets, stalled streams) as dominant symptom?
C1) Spike in “stream reset”/disconnect errors with otherwise healthy non-stream responses
  => incident_type: Streaming disconnects
     subtypes:
     - Gateway streaming reset
     - Proxy/load balancer idle timeout
     - Client SDK regression (if confirmed)
     - Network path instability (region/ISP)
Dashboards:
- Grafana: Streaming Health — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/streaming-health
Notes:
- Classification rule: default to platform-side streaming disconnects unless we have clear evidence it’s a client SDK regression.
- If confirmed client SDK issue (repro with specific SDK version; backend healthy), set subtype=Client SDK regression and tag Support owner as consulted.

D) Is the primary issue “we can’t see what’s happening” (missing metrics/logs), or the incident is driven by monitoring failure?
=> incident_type: Telemetry/Observability blind spot
   subtypes:
   - Missing/lagging metrics
   - Broken alert routing
   - Logging/trace pipeline outage
Dashboards:
- Grafana: Observability Pipeline — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/obs-pipeline

E) Is the primary issue around Private deployment connectivity/config (VPC/on-prem) rather than the hosted control plane/hosted runtime?
E1) Private customer cannot connect (DNS/TLS, tunnels, peering), or post-upgrade connectivity breaks
  => incident_type: Private deployment connectivity/config
     subtypes:
     - DNS resolution failure
     - TLS/cert chain failure
     - VPC peering / routing issue
     - Firewall / allowlist regression
     - Private upgrade/config mismatch
Dashboards:
- Grafana: Private Edge Connectivity — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/private-connectivity
Runbook pointers:
- Private Networking Triage — https://runbooks.redwood.internal/private/networking-triage

If none fit quickly
- Use incident_type: Unknown/Needs-triage
  - Add notes: “dominant symptom”, “most suspicious components”, and what you checked (dashboards/queries).
  - Re-evaluate after first mitigation or after 30 minutes.

Owner mapping reminder (not authoritative; incident-bot will auto-resolve)
- Capacity/Fleet shortage -> Infra/Fleet (consult Routing if failover/fallback involved)
- Routing/Fallback misbehavior -> Platform Routing (consult Infra/Fleet for capacity signals)
- Serving runtime failure/regression -> Serving Runtime (consult SRE if widespread)
- Control plane outage -> Platform Control Plane
- Auth/RBAC failure -> Security/Identity platform (Security Ops consulted)
- Streaming disconnects -> Edge/Gateway (consult Runtime if token generation stalls look like disconnects)
- Telemetry/Observability blind spot -> Observability
- Rollout/Release regression -> Owning service team of change (Runtime/Control Plane/Routing), SRE consulted for process
- Private deployment connectivity/config -> Private Deployments/Networking (consult Control Plane as needed)

Quick symptom-to-type cheat sheet
- “Users get 503s” -> Availability (start at A1a)
- “Everything is slow, mostly succeeds” -> Latency regression (B)
- “Streams disconnect / broken pipe” -> Streaming disconnects (C)
- “401/403 everywhere” -> Auth/RBAC failure (A3a)
- “429s during burst” -> Quota/Rate limiting vs Capacity (A2a)
- “Failover happened but traffic stuck / oscillating” -> Routing/Fallback misbehavior
- “Can’t update routes/policies” -> Control plane outage (even if serving OK)
- “No metrics, can’t tell” -> Telemetry blind spot (D)
- “Private customer can’t connect after upgrade” -> Private connectivity/config (E)

Examples (for calibration)
1) Elevated 5xx + router fallback reason=capacity + GPU saturation in us-east pool
- Primary: Capacity/Fleet shortage -> Regional capacity exhaustion
- Contributing: Routing (if fallback is expected and working, usually not primary)

2) Latency p99 doubles after runtime deploy; rollback restores
- Primary: Rollout/Release regression -> Runtime rollout regression
- Contributing: Serving runtime failure/regression (root mechanism belongs there, but primary classification for incident reporting is rollout regression in this case)

3) Streaming disconnect spike, non-stream endpoints healthy; edge proxy deploy 30 min earlier
- Primary: Streaming disconnects -> Gateway streaming reset
- Contributing: Rollout/Release regression (edge/gateway)

4) Dedicated customer sees 429s during burst; quota unchanged; GPU saturation high; queueing up
- Primary: Capacity/Fleet shortage -> Dedicated pool saturation (burst)
- Note: include Dedicated tier; capture mitigation (burst capacity / pool resize)

Open questions (parking lot for workshop)
- Boundary: When do we call something “Routing/Fallback misbehavior” vs “Capacity/Fleet shortage” when both are present? Current rule: whichever mitigation restored impact first, plus evidence (fallback_reason + capacity dashboards).
- Client vs platform streaming disconnects: do we want an explicit “Client SDK regression” top-level type later, or keep as Streaming subtype?
- Dedicated/Private carve-outs: how do we annotate incidents that are primarily customer-caused misconfig (still trackable but excluded from SLA timers)?

Changelog
- 2025-11-26: initial draft (Rafael)
- 2025-12-03: added routing/fallback structured reasons + dashboard links (Mina/Allison feedback)
- 2025-12-18: added Private connectivity branch + runbook link (Victor feedback)
- 2026-01-06: clarified multi-cause rule + 429 guidance for Dedicated burst (Vanessa/Dev feedback)
