- “Private customer can’t connect after upgrade” -> Private connectivity/config (E)

Examples (for calibration)
1) Elevated 5xx + router fallback reason=capacity + GPU saturation in us-east pool
- Primary: Capacity/Fleet shortage -> Regional capacity exhaustion
- Contributing: Routing (if fallback is expected and working, usually not primary)

2) Latency p99 doubles after runtime deploy; rollback restores
- Primary: Rollout/Release regression -> Runtime rollout regression
- Contributing: Serving runtime failure/regression (root mechanism belongs there, but primary classification for incident reporting is rollout regression in this case)

3) Streaming disconnect spike, non-stream endpoints healthy; edge proxy deploy 30 min earlier
- Primary: Streaming disconnects -> Gateway streaming reset
- Contributing: Rollout/Release regression (edge/gateway)

4) Dedicated customer sees 429s during burst; quota unchanged; GPU saturation high; queueing up
- Primary: Capacity/Fleet shortage -> Dedicated pool saturation (burst)
- Note: include Dedicated tier; capture mitigation (burst capacity / pool resize)

Open questions (parking lot for workshop)
- Boundary: When do we call something “Routing/Fallback misbehavior” vs “Capacity/Fleet shortage” when both are present? Current rule: whichever mitigation restored impact first, plus evidence (fallback_reason + capacity dashboards).
- Client vs platform streaming disconnects: do we want an explicit “Client SDK regression” top-level type later, or keep as Streaming subtype?
- Dedicated/Private carve-outs: how do we annotate incidents that are primarily customer-caused misconfig (still trackable but excluded from SLA timers)?

Changelog
- 2025-11-26: initial draft (Rafael)
- 2025-12-03: added routing/fallback structured reasons + dashboard links (Mina/Allison feedback)
- 2025-12-18: added Private connectivity branch + runbook link (Victor feedback)
- 2026-01-06: clarified multi-cause rule + 429 guidance for Dedicated burst (Vanessa/Dev feedback)
text:
Purpose
- Provide a fast, consistent incident_type + subtype selection during live incidents.
- Optimize for “good enough in 2–5 minutes” classification that can be refined later.
- Keep v1 low-cardinality: choose exactly 1 primary incident_type/subtype; list other contributing areas separately (postmortem / incident-bot contributing_systems).

Scope reminders (v1)
- In-scope: availability (5xx/overload), latency regressions, streaming disconnects, routing/fallback misbehavior, capacity/fleet shortages, control plane outages, auth/RBAC failures, telemetry/metrics blind spots, private deployment connectivity/config issues.
- Out-of-scope: deep RCA framework (belongs in postmortem), long-term roadmap changes not tied to incident reduction.

How to use this during an incident
1) Identify customer tier(s) impacted: Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private.
2) Confirm the dominant symptom from user perspective (errors vs slow vs disconnect vs cannot authenticate vs cannot change config).
3) Pick the best matching incident_type/subtype below.
4) Set incident_type + subtype in incident-bot ASAP (target: within 15 minutes of incident declaration). Update later if new evidence changes the primary driver.

Incident-bot workflow (current)
- /incident set-type  (select from dropdown; subtype required)
- /incident set-tier  (Hosted/Dedicated/Private/multi)
- /incident set-owner (auto-resolved from type/subtype; verify)
- If you truly cannot decide: set incident_type=Unknown/Needs-triage, add a note, and set a reminder to re-evaluate in 30 minutes.

Rule for multi-cause incidents (important)
- Choose the primary incident_type based on what first restored customer impact when mitigated.
  - Example: capacity shortage caused overload, but a routing policy bug prevented fallback. If fixing routing immediately restored success rate, primary = Routing/Fallback misbehavior; capacity becomes contributing.
  - If adding capacity immediately restored success rate and routing behaved as expected, primary = Capacity/Fleet shortage; routing becomes contributing.

Decision tree (quick checks)

START: Is there confirmed customer impact?
- Yes: proceed.
- Unclear: if only internal alerts without user impact, still classify if Sev0/Sev1 internal reliability incident; otherwise track as degraded signal / investigation.

A) Are we seeing elevated error rates (4xx/5xx) for inference requests?
A1) 5xx spike (or gateway 502/503/504) is the dominant symptom
  -> Go to A1a
A2) 429 spike is the dominant symptom
  -> Go to A2a
A3) Mostly 401/403 authentication/authorization errors
  -> Go to A3a
A4) Mostly client-side errors, but strongly correlated with streaming disconnects (e.g., broken pipe, reset)
  -> Go to C (Streaming)

A1a) 5xx dominant: identify where errors originate
- If errors concentrated in one region/cluster and correlate with “no capacity” / saturation signals
  => incident_type: Capacity/Fleet shortage
     subtypes (pick one):
     - Regional capacity exhaustion (GPU saturation)
     - Provisioning lag / scale-out delay
     - Fleet health degradation (node failures/draining)
     - Quota misconfiguration causing effective capacity drop (if 5xx, not 429)
  Primary dashboards:
  - Grafana: Fleet Capacity Overview (SRE) — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-capacity-overview
  - Grafana: GPU Saturation by Pool — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/gpu-saturation
  Common mitigations:
  - Add/burst capacity; shed load; raise pool limits; move traffic; disable expensive features; coordinate region shift.

- If router fallback rate spikes and “fallback_reason” suggests policy/health misbehavior (not just capacity)
  => incident_type: Routing/Fallback misbehavior
     subtypes:
     - Fallback loop / oscillation
     - Incorrect policy evaluation (routing config)
     - Health signal mismatch (false unhealthy/healthy)
     - Region failover misbehavior (stuck, partial, flapping)
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Routing + Fallback Reasons — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/router-fallback
  - Grafana: Region Failover Health — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/region-failover
  Notes:
  - If cloud provider outage triggers failover, still classify based on our system behavior; add “external_dependency” in notes.

- If 5xx correlates with a recent release/rollout (control plane or runtime) and rollback fixes it
  => incident_type: Rollout/Release regression
     subtypes:
     - Runtime rollout regression
     - Router/control-plane rollout regression
     - Config rollout regression
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Deployments + Error Overlay — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/deploy-overlay
  Mitigations:
  - Roll back/canary halt; pin model/runtime version; revert config.

- If 5xx is caused by control plane request failures (e.g., cannot create/update routes; config write failures) OR widespread config fetch failures
  => incident_type: Control plane outage
     subtypes:
     - API errors (read path)
     - API errors (write path)
     - Config propagation failure
     - Control plane dependency outage (DB/queue)
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Control Plane SLO — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/control-plane-slo
  Notes:
  - If inference serving is fine but customers cannot change config, still treat as customer-impacting (especially Dedicated/Private).

- If 5xx is due to serving runtime failures (OOM, scheduler stall, KV cache thrash) and shows up in runtime metrics/logs
  => incident_type: Serving runtime failure/regression
     subtypes:
     - OOM / memory fragmentation
     - KV cache thrash / eviction storm
     - Scheduler stalls / deadlocks
     - Token generation stalls / timeouts
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Runtime Health (per model) — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/runtime-health
  - Logs: Runtime error signatures — https://logs.redwood.internal/s/runtime-errors

- If you cannot attribute errors because observability is missing/incorrect (no metrics/log gaps)
  => incident_type: Telemetry/Observability blind spot
     subtypes:
     - Metrics ingestion delay/drop
     - Logging pipeline failure
     - Alerting misconfiguration / false positives
     - Dashboard query failure/permission
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Observability Pipeline — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/obs-pipeline

A2a) 429 dominant: throttling vs saturation vs policy
- If 429s align with documented quota/rate limit behavior and recent config change
  => incident_type: Quota/Rate limiting enforcement
     subtypes:
     - Misconfigured quota
     - Unexpected rate limiter behavior
     - Account-level enforcement bug
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Rate Limiter — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/rate-limiter

- If 429s appear during bursts on Dedicated and capacity is saturated (GPU near 100%, queueing high)
  => incident_type: Capacity/Fleet shortage
     subtype: Dedicated pool saturation (burst)
  Note:
  - We still classify as capacity if the system is correctly protecting itself, but add customer tier and “expected protection vs unexpected” in notes.

A3a) 401/403 dominant
=> incident_type: Auth/RBAC failure
   subtypes:
   - Token validation failure
   - RBAC policy evaluation failure
   - Key rotation / KMS integration issue
   - Identity provider dependency outage
Dashboards:
- Grafana: Auth + RBAC Errors — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/auth-rbac
Notes:
- If suspicious pattern (credential stuffing, anomalous geo) loop in Security Ops and dual-track as security incident per policy.

B) If not errors: are we seeing latency regressions (p95/p99) with success rate mostly stable?
B1) Latency elevated across multiple models/routes/regions
  -> Go to B1a
B2) Latency elevated for a specific model version or after model rollout
  -> Go to B2a
B3) Latency elevated but metrics are inconsistent/untrusted
  -> Go to D (Telemetry)

B1a) Broad latency regression
- If queueing time increases and GPU saturation is high
  => incident_type: Capacity/Fleet shortage
     subtype: Saturation-driven latency (queueing)
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Queueing + Concurrency — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/serving-queue

- If router is sending traffic to higher-latency region/model unexpectedly (policy/routing)
  => incident_type: Routing/Fallback misbehavior
     subtype: Policy-induced latency regression
  Dashboards:
  - Grafana: Route Decision Breakdown — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/route-decisions

- If runtime-level regressions correlate with a build/version change (scheduler/KV cache)
  => incident_type: Serving runtime failure/regression
     subtype: Throughput regression (runtime)

B2a) Latency regression tied to a rollout/config change
=> incident_type: Rollout/Release regression
   subtype: Model/version rollout regression (latency)
Dashboards:
- Grafana: Canary / Perf Overlays — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/perf-canary

C) Are we seeing streaming issues (disconnects, resets, stalled streams) as dominant symptom?
C1) Spike in “stream reset”/disconnect errors with otherwise healthy non-stream responses
  => incident_type: Streaming disconnects
     subtypes:
     - Gateway streaming reset
     - Proxy/load balancer idle timeout
     - Client SDK regression (if confirmed)
     - Network path instability (region/ISP)
Dashboards:
- Grafana: Streaming Health — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/streaming-health
Notes:
- Classification rule: default to platform-side streaming disconnects unless we have clear evidence it’s a client SDK regression.
- If confirmed client SDK issue (repro with specific SDK version; backend healthy), set subtype=Client SDK regression and tag Support owner as consulted.

D) Is the primary issue “we can’t see what’s happening” (missing metrics/logs), or the incident is driven by monitoring failure?
=> incident_type: Telemetry/Observability blind spot
   subtypes:
   - Missing/lagging metrics
   - Broken alert routing
   - Logging/trace pipeline outage
Dashboards:
- Grafana: Observability Pipeline — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/obs-pipeline

E) Is the primary issue around Private deployment connectivity/config (VPC/on-prem) rather than the hosted control plane/hosted runtime?
E1) Private customer cannot connect (DNS/TLS, tunnels, peering), or post-upgrade connectivity breaks
  => incident_type: Private deployment connectivity/config
     subtypes:
     - DNS resolution failure
     - TLS/cert chain failure
     - VPC peering / routing issue
     - Firewall / allowlist regression
     - Private upgrade/config mismatch
Dashboards:
- Grafana: Private Edge Connectivity — https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/private-connectivity
Runbook pointers:
- Private Networking Triage — https://runbooks.redwood.internal/private/networking-triage

If none fit quickly
- Use incident_type: Unknown/Needs-triage
  - Add notes: “dominant symptom”, “most suspicious components”, and what you checked (dashboards/queries).
  - Re-evaluate after first mitigation or after 30 minutes.

Owner mapping reminder (not authoritative; incident-bot will auto-resolve)
- Capacity/Fleet shortage -> Infra/Fleet (consult Routing if failover/fallback involved)
- Routing/Fallback misbehavior -> Platform Routing (consult Infra/Fleet for capacity signals)
- Serving runtime failure/regression -> Serving Runtime (consult SRE if widespread)
- Control plane outage -> Platform Control Plane
- Auth/RBAC failure -> Security/Identity platform (Security Ops consulted)
- Streaming disconnects -> Edge/Gateway (consult Runtime if token generation stalls look like disconnects)
- Telemetry/Observability blind spot -> Observability
- Rollout/Release regression -> Owning service team of change (Runtime/Control Plane/Routing), SRE consulted for process
- Private deployment connectivity/config -> Private Deployments/Networking (consult Control Plane as needed)

Quick symptom-to-type cheat sheet
- “Users get 503s” -> Availability (start at A1a)
- “Everything is slow, mostly succeeds” -> Latency regression (B)
- “Streams disconnect / broken pipe” -> Streaming disconnects (C)
- “401/403 everywhere” -> Auth/RBAC failure (A3a)
- “429s during burst” -> Quota/Rate limiting vs Capacity (A2a)
- “Failover happened but traffic stuck / oscillating” -> Routing/Fallback misbehavior
- “Can’t update routes/policies” -> Control plane outage (even if serving OK)
- “No metrics, can’t tell” -> Telemetry blind spot (D)
- “Private customer can’t connect after upgrade” -> Private connectivity/config (E)

Examples (for calibration)
1) Elevated 5xx + router fallback reason=capacity + GPU saturation in us-east pool
- Primary: Capacity/Fleet shortage -> Regional capacity exhaustion
- Contributing: Routing (if fallback is expected and working, usually not primary)

2) Latency p99 doubles after runtime deploy; rollback restores
- Primary: Rollout/Release regression -> Runtime rollout regression
- Contributing: Serving runtime failure/regression (root mechanism belongs there, but primary classification for incident reporting is rollout regression in this case)

3) Streaming disconnect spike, non-stream endpoints healthy; edge proxy deploy 30 min earlier
- Primary: Streaming disconnects -> Gateway streaming reset
- Contributing: Rollout/Release regression (edge/gateway)

4) Dedicated customer sees 429s during burst; quota unchanged; GPU saturation high; queueing up
- Primary: Capacity/Fleet shortage -> Dedicated pool saturation (burst)
- Note: include Dedicated tier; capture mitigation (burst capacity / pool resize)

Open questions (parking lot for workshop)
- Boundary: When do we call something “Routing/Fallback misbehavior” vs “Capacity/Fleet shortage” when both are present? Current rule: whichever mitigation restored impact first, plus evidence (fallback_reason + capacity dashboards).
- Client vs platform streaming disconnects: do we want an explicit “Client SDK regression” top-level type later, or keep as Streaming subtype?
- Dedicated/Private carve-outs: how do we annotate incidents that are primarily customer-caused misconfig (still trackable but excluded from SLA timers)?

Changelog
- 2025-11-26: initial draft (Rafael)
- 2025-12-03: added routing/fallback structured reasons + dashboard links (Mina/Allison feedback)
- 2025-12-18: added Private connectivity branch + runbook link (Victor feedback)
- 2026-01-06: clarified multi-cause rule + 429 guidance for Dedicated burst (Vanessa/Dev feedback)

source.title: Incident taxonomy v1 workshop notes (Dec 2025)
source.content:
Workshop: Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping (v1)
Date/time: 2025-12-03 10:00–11:30 PT
Location: Zoom (SRE Reliability Program)
Facilitator: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
Notetaker: Mei Lin (SRE)

Goal of session
- Converge on a low-cardinality, on-call-friendly v1 taxonomy (types + key subtypes)
- Agree on classification decision rules for common ambiguous cases
- Identify ownership mapping hot spots to resolve async (Routing vs Fleet, Runtime vs Telemetry)
- Capture follow-ups for bot/tooling + dashboards + SLA policy alignment

Attendees
- SRE: Rafael Mendes (program lead), Aisha Bello (SLO/SLA dashboards)
- Observability: Allison Grant (standards), Sergio Costa (dashboards) [joined 25 min]
- Data/analysis: Mei Lin (incident analysis)
- Platform/Control Plane: Ethan Park
- Routing: Mina Haddad, Ravi Menon
- Infra/Fleet: Vanessa Ortiz, Harish Reddy
- Private connectivity/networking: Victor Alvarez
- Serving runtime: Sofia Ivanova, Noah Patel
- Security ops: Fiona McCarthy
- Support/CS: Dev Patel
- Tooling: Dylan Brooks, Devon Price
- Program mgmt: Talia Benson

Pre-read (sent 12/01)
- Top incidents analysis (last 2 quarters) + candidate clusters
- Draft decision tree (v0.3)
- Draft taxonomy list (10–15 categories) + definitions in Confluence draft

Agenda
1) 10 min: framing + constraints (cardinality + reporting + on-call use)
2) 35 min: walk through proposed top-level types and definitions
3) 25 min: ambiguous incident examples (capacity vs routing; runtime vs telemetry)
4) 10 min: ownership mapping principles + escalation
5) 10 min: follow-ups + timeline for v1 signoff

Notes / discussion highlights

Constraints everyone aligned on
- We want something an IC can choose in <60 seconds during a live incident.
- Keep it stable and low-cardinality. Avoid turning incident_type into “freeform tags in disguise”.
- We need one primary owner per incident for accountability + reporting, but allow contributing systems in postmortems.
- Metrics labeling: allowlist only; unknown/unclassified must be an explicit bucket.

Proposed top-level incident types (v1) discussed
(Names are working; we agreed on “nouny” names that map to symptoms/alerts.)

A) Availability / elevated 5xx
- Includes: request errors at gateway/router/serving path resulting in 5xx/failed responses
- Excludes: client-side failures; expected throttling (429) unless it’s caused by platform misbehavior

B) Latency regression (p95/p99)
- Includes: sustained latency increase above SLO, even if still mostly 2xx
- Subtype candidates:
  - runtime performance regression
  - routing/region selection regression
  - upstream dependency latency (control plane, auth, storage)

C) Streaming disconnects
- Includes: SSE/WebSocket streaming resets, partial responses, elevated disconnect rate
- Boundary: if it’s clearly client SDK bug, we still classify as Streaming but mark “customer-caused” in contributing factors (for reporting + comms), per Dev/Support request.

D) Smart routing / fallback misbehavior
- Includes: fallback loops, wrong target, policy violations, region/model pin not honored, fallback_reason mismatch
- Important: do not overload this category for simple “we’re out of GPUs” cases.

E) Capacity / fleet shortage
- Includes: GPU saturation, provisioning lag, inability to scale to demand, regional scarcity
- Excludes: cases where capacity exists but router is selecting wrong pool (Routing misbehavior)

F) Control plane outage / degradation
- Includes: inability to read/write config (routing policy updates failing), rollout controls broken, console/API management endpoints failing

G) Auth / RBAC failures
- Includes: authn/authz outages, token validation failures, permission errors due to platform change
- Boundary: suspicious patterns may trigger dual-tracking with Security.

H) Telemetry / metrics blind spots
- Includes: missing/incorrect metrics/logs/traces that prevent detection or response; alerting pipeline broken
- Note: We debated whether this should be an “incident type” or “contributing factor.” Outcome below.

I) Private deployment connectivity / config issues
- Includes: VPC/on-prem specific: DNS/TLS, routing between customer network and Redwood endpoints, misconfig after upgrade
- Ownership split between Private Deployments/Infra vs “core platform” depending on boundary. Outcome below.

J) Rollout/config regression
- “Rollout regression” felt important enough to encode (many recent incidents tied to model version rollouts + config changes).
- Debate: make it subtype under Availability/Latency vs a top-level type.

Decisions made live (v1)

1) Keep v1 to 10 top-level types max.
- Decision: cap at 10 top-level types; subtypes only where it improves routing/ownership and reduces ambiguity.
- Rationale: reporting + metrics labeling + on-call choice simplicity.

2) Telemetry/metrics blind spots stays as a top-level type in v1.
- Decision: keep “Telemetry/observability impairment” as a top-level incident type.
- Rationale: we have high-severity internal incidents where we lose visibility and materially increase MTTR; needs explicit tracking and ownership.
- Add note in decision tree: if customer impact is primarily availability/latency, choose that as primary type and record telemetry as contributing factor (postmortem). If the primary impact is inability to detect/triage (e.g., pager not firing, missing error metrics), choose Telemetry.
- Owner expectation: Observability team primary (Allison to confirm exact owning service list).

3) Rollout/config regression is a subtype, not a top-level type.
- Decision: do NOT add “Rollout regression” as its own top-level type.
- Instead: add a consistent subtype under Availability and Latency: “Rollout/config regression”.
- Rationale: keeps top-level categories symptom-oriented; rollout is a cause/mechanism.
- Required postmortem field: “triggering_change” (deploy, config, model version, infra change) to capture rollout context.

4) Capacity vs routing primary classification rule
- Decision rule:
  - If capacity alerts show saturation/scarcity and router is behaving as designed (fallback_reason=capacity, increased queueing, provisioning lag), classify as Capacity/Fleet shortage (primary owner: Fleet).
  - If capacity exists OR fallback_reason indicates policy/health mismatch OR traffic is routed away from healthy capacity, classify as Routing/fallback misbehavior (primary owner: Routing).
- Ravi noted we need structured fallback reasons to make this rule apply consistently (ties to ENG-4824).

5) Private connectivity categories: keep as one top-level with scoped subtypes
- Decision: keep “Private deployment connectivity/config” as a single top-level type, but add subtypes to prevent it becoming a catch-all:
  - DNS/TLS
  - Customer network/VPC routing
  - Private gateway/edge
  - Upgrade/migration regression (private package)
- Owner principle: if issue is in customer network, still primary owner is Private Deployments/Networking SME for mitigation guidance + comms; mark “customer-caused” for SLA/credits logic.

6) Security alignment / dual-tracking trigger
- Decision: add explicit trigger in classification policy:
  - If auth failures appear suspicious (credential stuffing, unusual geo/IP, repeated token failures), open Security incident in parallel.
  - Reliability incident remains primary for service restoration; security incident tracks investigation/audit.
- Fiona to provide a 1-pager for handoff triggers and required audit log links.

7) Ownership mapping principle: one primary owner by “operational control boundary”
- Decision: primary owner should be the team that can mitigate fastest (feature flags, rollbacks, routing changes, capacity moves), not necessarily who wrote the original code.
- Postmortems capture contributing teams/systems.

Draft taxonomy list (post-workshop edits to incorporate)
- Availability (5xx / error rate)
  - subtypes: overload/queueing, upstream dependency failure, rollout/config regression
- Latency regression
  - subtypes: runtime perf regression, routing/regional selection regression, upstream dependency latency, rollout/config regression
- Streaming disconnects
  - subtypes: gateway/edge resets, client SDK compatibility, network instability (private), runtime streaming bug
- Routing/fallback misbehavior
  - subtypes: policy misconfig, health signal mismatch, fallback loop/wrong target, pinning/affinity violation
- Capacity/fleet shortage
  - subtypes: regional GPU scarcity, provisioning lag, autoscaler failure, quota vs capacity confusion
- Control plane outage/degradation
  - subtypes: config write failure, config propagation delay, rollout controller failure, internal API outage
- Auth/RBAC failures
  - subtypes: token validation, RBAC policy regression, dependency outage (IdP/KMS)
- Telemetry/observability impairment
  - subtypes: metrics pipeline, logs pipeline, tracing pipeline, alert routing/paging
- Private deployment connectivity/config
  - subtypes: DNS/TLS, VPC routing, private gateway/edge, private upgrade regression
- Data plane dependency degradation (tentative)
  - This is the only one we did not finalize naming for. It covers “not control plane, but dependencies like model registry/storage/KV cache backing services” (if they exist). Parking lot item below.

Parking lot / unresolved questions
1) “Data plane dependency degradation” naming and whether it’s needed in v1.
- We have incidents where the serving path is fine but a dependency (artifact fetch, model registry, etc.) causes failures.
- Option A: keep as subtypes under Availability/Latency (upstream dependency failure/latency)
- Option B: make a distinct type for dependencies to cluster work.
- Action: Rafael + Aisha to review last 90 days: do we have enough volume to justify? If not, collapse into Availability/Latency subtypes.

2) 429s / throttling classification
- Dedicated customers frequently escalate 429s.
- Need consistent rule for “expected quota enforcement” vs “capacity saturation leading to throttling.”
- Tentative: 429 where quotas are correct → not an incident unless it’s a regression/misconfig; 429 due to saturation → Capacity/Fleet (or Routing if misrouted).
- Action: Vanessa + Dev Patel to draft wording for decision tree + comms template.

3) Multi-cause incidents (capacity + routing + runtime)
- We need a default: pick the “first mitigatable” primary type; record others as contributing systems.
- Action: add examples to the decision tree and postmortem template.

4) Metrics label cardinality
- Observability wants allowlist + sampling rules; concern that subtypes will explode.
- Action: Allison to propose final allowlist format; Dylan/Devon to implement gating in incident-bot UI so we can’t create novel values.

5) SLA timers: when do they start/stop
- Need crisp definition: “mitigation achieved” vs “fully resolved” and how we handle partial region restores.
- Action: Aisha + Product stakeholders (Nikhil/Lauren/Vivek) to align (separate meeting already planned).

Follow-up actions (assigned)

Taxonomy + decision tree
- [Rafael] Update Confluence draft taxonomy with workshop decisions + subtype list (due 2025-12-06)
- [Mei] Add 6–8 real incident examples mapped to categories (incl. capacity vs routing, streaming, private DNS/TLS) (due 2025-12-09)
- [Kira] Update decision tree draft with the new rules + “telemetry as primary vs contributing factor” guidance (due 2025-12-10)

Ownership mapping
- [Mina + Vanessa] Resolve the disputed cases list (capacity vs routing) and propose one primary owner per subtype (due 2025-12-11)
- [Ethan] Confirm control plane subtypes mapping (config write failures vs propagation) + escalation channel naming (due 2025-12-11)
- [Victor] Propose owner split for Private subtypes + which runbooks exist vs need writing (due 2025-12-12)

Tooling integration
- [Dylan + Devon] Incident-bot: required incident_type + subtype dropdown + “unknown” flow (ENG-4822) and closure gating for Sev0/Sev1 (target start 2025-12-09)
- [Allison] Provide metric label allowlist + sampling guidance for incident_type emission (ENG-4826 input) (due 2025-12-13)
- [Sergio] Draft first dashboard mock panels using the new taxonomy buckets (ENG-4828) (due 2025-12-16)
- [Mina] Routing: structured fallback_reason mapping notes to taxonomy (ENG-4824 input) (due 2025-12-13)

SLA alignment / comms
- [Aisha] Draft remediation SLA matrix v0 and identify where Dedicated/Private differ (sync with PM ticket PM-912) (due 2025-12-15)
- [Dev Patel] Draft Support/CS guidance snippet: how to communicate incident “type” without over-claiming root cause (due 2025-12-16)
- [Fiona] Security alignment: triggers for dual-tracking + audit expectations for auth-related incidents (due 2025-12-17)

Next milestones
- 12/06: taxonomy definitions updated in Confluence draft
- 12/10: decision tree updated + examples
- 12/12: ownership mapping draft circulated for review
- Week of 12/15: signoff round + begin incident-bot required fields rollout (pilot)

Open concerns captured (verbatim-ish)
- “If we make subtypes too granular, we’ll never get clean dashboards.” (Observability)
- “Primary owner needs to match who can actually mitigate at 2am, not who built it.” (SRE)
- “Support needs a consistent statement: what category it is, what mitigations are happening, what’s next update time.” (CS)

Appendix: disputed examples we walked through
1) Regional GPU event: elevated 5xx + increased fallback
- Data: fallback_reason=capacity, GPU utilization pegged, provisioning queue growing
- Decision: classify as Capacity/Fleet shortage (primary), contributing: Routing (if fallback behavior increases latency)

2) Latency regression after model rollout
- Data: p99 +40%, no error increase, correlates with model version X.Y
- Decision: Latency regression → subtype rollout/config regression; contributing: Runtime

3) Streaming resets spike after SDK upgrade (customer)
- Data: disconnect rate up, only a subset of clients, server metrics stable
- Decision: Streaming disconnects (primary), contributing: client SDK compatibility; mark customer-caused if validated

4) Control plane config write failures
- Data: customers cannot update routing policies; data plane serving still working
- Decision: Control plane outage/degradation (primary)

title: Incident taxonomy v1 workshop notes (Dec 2025)
preview:
Workshop: Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping (v1)
Date/time: 2025-12-03 10:00–11:30 PT
Location: Zoom (SRE Reliability Program)
Facilitator: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
Notetaker: Mei Lin (SRE)

Goal of session
- Converge on a low-cardinality, on-call-friendly v1 taxonomy (types + key subtypes)
- Agree on classification decision rules for common ambiguous cases
- Identify ownership mapping hot spots to resolve async (Routing vs Fleet, Runtime vs Telemetry)
- Capture follow-ups for bot/tooling + dashboards + SLA policy alignment

Attendees
- SRE: Rafael Mendes (program lead), Aisha Bello (SLO/SLA dashboards)
- Observability: Allison Grant (standards), Sergio Costa (dashboards) [joined 25 min]
- Data/analysis: Mei Lin (incident analysis)
- Platform/Control Plane: Ethan Park
- Routing: Mina Haddad, Ravi Menon
- Infra/Fleet: Vanessa Ortiz, Harish Reddy
- Private connectivity/networking: Victor Alvarez
- Serving runtime: Sofia Ivanova, Noah Patel
- Security ops: Fiona McCarthy
- Support/CS: Dev Patel
- Tooling: Dylan Brooks, Devon Price
- Program mgmt: Talia Benson

Pre-read (sent 12/01)
- Top incidents analysis (last 2 quarters) + candidate clusters
- Draft decision tree (v0.3)
- Draft taxonomy list (10–15 categories) + definitions in Confluence draft

Agenda
1) 10 min: framing + constraints (cardinality + reporting + on-call use)
2) 35 min: walk through proposed top-level types and definitions
3) 25 min: ambiguous incident examples (capacity vs routing; runtime vs telemetry)
4) 10 min: ownership mapping principles + escalation
5) 10 min: follow-ups + timeline for v1 signoff

Notes / discussion highlights

Constraints everyone aligned on
- We want something an IC can choose in <60 seconds during a live incident.
- Keep it stable and low-cardinality. Avoid turning incident_type into “freeform tags in disguise”.
- We need one primary owner per incident for accountability + reporting, but allow contributing systems in postmortems.
- Metrics labeling: allowlist only; unknown/unclassified must be an explicit bucket.

Proposed top-level incident types (v1) discussed
(Names are working; we agreed on “nouny” names that map to symptoms/alerts.)

A) Availability / elevated 5xx
- Includes: request errors at gateway/router/serving path resulting in 5xx/failed responses
- Excludes: client-side failures; expected throttling (429) unless it’s caused by platform misbehavior

B) Latency regression (p95/p99)
- Includes: sustained latency increase above SLO, even if still mostly 2xx
- Subtype candidates:
  - runtime performance regression
  - routing/region selection regression
  - upstream dependency latency (control plane, auth, storage)

C) Streaming disconnects
- Includes: SSE/WebSocket streaming resets, partial responses, elevated disconnect rate
- Boundary: if it’s clearly client SDK bug, we still classify as Streaming but mark “customer-caused” in contributing factors (for reporting + comms), per Dev/Support request.

D) Smart routing / fallback misbehavior
- Includes: fallback loops, wrong target, policy violations, region/model pin not honored, fallback_reason mismatch
- Important: do not overload this category for simple “we’re out of GPUs” cases.

E) Capacity / fleet shortage
- Includes: GPU saturation, provisioning lag, inability to scale to demand, regional scarcity
- Excludes: cases where capacity exists but router is selecting wrong pool (Routing misbehavior)

F) Control plane outage / degradation
- Includes: inability to read/write config (routing policy updates failing), rollout controls broken, console/API management endpoints failing

G) Auth / RBAC failures
- Includes: authn/authz outages, token validation failures, permission errors due to platform change
- Boundary: suspicious patterns may trigger dual-tracking with Security.

H) Telemetry / metrics blind spots
- Includes: missing/incorrect metrics/logs/traces that prevent detection or response; alerting pipeline broken
- Note: We debated whether this should be an “incident type” or “contributing factor.” Outcome below.

I) Private deployment connectivity / config issues
- Includes: VPC/on-prem specific: DNS/TLS, routing between customer network and Redwood endpoints, misconfig after upgrade
- Ownership split between Private Deployments/Infra vs “core platform” depending on boundary. Outcome below.

J) Rollout/config regression
- “Rollout regression” felt important enough to encode (many recent incidents tied to model version rollouts + config changes).
- Debate: make it subtype under Availability/Latency vs a top-level type.

Decisions made live (v1)

1) Keep v1 to 10 top-level types max.
- Decision: cap at 10 top-level types; subtypes only where it improves routing/ownership and reduces ambiguity.
- Rationale: reporting + metrics labeling + on-call choice simplicity.

2) Telemetry/metrics blind spots stays as a top-level type in v1.
- Decision: keep “Telemetry/observability impairment” as a top-level incident type.
- Rationale: we have high-severity internal incidents where we lose visibility and materially increase MTTR; needs explicit tracking and ownership.
- Add note in decision tree: if customer impact is primarily availability/latency, choose that as primary type and record telemetry as contributing factor (postmortem). If the primary impact is inability to detect/triage (e.g., pager not firing, missing error metrics), choose Telemetry.
- Owner expectation: Observability team primary (Allison to confirm exact owning service list).

3) Rollout/config regression is a subtype, not a top-level type.
- Decision: do NOT add “Rollout regression” as its own top-level type.
- Instead: add a consistent subtype under Availability and Latency: “Rollout/config regression”.
- Rationale: keeps top-level categories symptom-oriented; rollout is a cause/mechanism.
- Required postmortem field: “triggering_change” (deploy, config, model version, infra change) to capture rollout context.

4) Capacity vs routing primary classification rule
- Decision rule:
  - If capacity alerts show saturation/scarcity and router is behaving as designed (fallback_reason=capacity, increased queueing, provisioning lag), classify as Capacity/Fleet shortage (primary owner: Fleet).
  - If capacity exists OR fallback_reason indicates policy/health mismatch OR traffic is routed away from healthy capacity, classify as Routing/fallback misbehavior (primary owner: Routing).
- Ravi noted we need structured fallback reasons to make this rule apply consistently (ties to ENG-4824).

5) Private connectivity categories: keep as one top-level with scoped subtypes
- Decision: keep “Private deployment connectivity/config” as a single top-level type, but add subtypes to prevent it becoming a catch-all:
  - DNS/TLS
  - Customer network/VPC routing
  - Private gateway/edge
  - Upgrade/migration regression (private package)
- Owner principle: if issue is in customer network, still primary owner is Private Deployments/Networking SME for mitigation guidance + comms; mark “customer-caused” for SLA/credits logic.

6) Security alignment / dual-tracking trigger
- Decision: add explicit trigger in classification policy:
  - If auth failures appear suspicious (credential stuffing, unusual geo/IP, repeated token failures), open Security incident in parallel.
  - Reliability incident remains primary for service restoration; security incident tracks investigation/audit.
- Fiona to provide a 1-pager for handoff triggers and required audit log links.

7) Ownership mapping principle: one primary owner by “operational control boundary”
- Decision: primary owner should be the team that can mitigate fastest (feature flags, rollbacks, routing changes, capacity moves), not necessarily who wrote the original code.
- Postmortems capture contributing teams/systems.

Draft taxonomy list (post-workshop edits to incorporate)
- Availability (5xx / error rate)
  - subtypes: overload/queueing, upstream dependency failure, rollout/config regression
- Latency regression
  - subtypes: runtime perf regression, routing/regional selection regression, upstream dependency latency, rollout/config regression
- Streaming disconnects
  - subtypes: gateway/edge resets, client SDK compatibility, network instability (private), runtime streaming bug
- Routing/fallback misbehavior
  - subtypes: policy misconfig, health signal mismatch, fallback loop/wrong target, pinning/affinity violation
- Capacity/fleet shortage
  - subtypes: regional GPU scarcity, provisioning lag, autoscaler failure, quota vs capacity confusion
- Control plane outage/degradation
  - subtypes: config write failure, config propagation delay, rollout controller failure, internal API outage
- Auth/RBAC failures
  - subtypes: token validation, RBAC policy regression, dependency outage (IdP/KMS)
- Telemetry/observability impairment
  - subtypes: metrics pipeline, logs pipeline, tracing pipeline, alert routing/paging
- Private deployment connectivity/config
  - subtypes: DNS/TLS, VPC routing, private gateway/edge, private upgrade regression
- Data plane dependency degradation (tentative)
  - This is the only one we did not finalize naming for. It covers “not control plane, but dependencies like model registry/storage/KV cache backing services” (if they exist). Parking lot item below.

Parking lot / unresolved questions
1) “Data plane dependency degradation” naming and whether it’s needed in v1.
- We have incidents where the serving path is fine but a dependency (artifact fetch, model registry, etc.) causes failures.
- Option A: keep as subtypes under Availability/Latency (upstream dependency failure/latency)
- Option B: make a distinct type for dependencies to cluster work.
- Action: Rafael + Aisha to review last 90 days: do we have enough volume to justify? If not, collapse into Availability/Latency subtypes.

2) 429s / throttling classification
- Dedicated customers frequently escalate 429s.
- Need consistent rule for “expected quota enforcement” vs “capacity saturation leading to throttling.”
- Tentative: 429 where quotas are correct → not an incident unless it’s a regression/misconfig; 429 due to saturation → Capacity/Fleet (or Routing if misrouted).
- Action: Vanessa + Dev Patel to draft wording for decision tree + comms template.

3) Multi-cause incidents (capacity + routing + runtime)
- We need a default: pick the “first mitigatable” primary type; record others as contributing systems.
- Action: add examples to the decision tree and postmortem template.

4) Metrics label cardinality
- Observability wants allowlist + sampling rules; concern that subtypes will explode.
- Action: Allison to propose final allowlist format; Dylan/Devon to implement gating in incident-bot UI so we can’t create novel values.

5) SLA timers: when do they start/stop
- Need crisp definition: “mitigation achieved” vs “fully resolved” and how we handle partial region restores.
- Action: Aisha + Product stakeholders (Nikhil/Lauren/Vivek) to align (separate meeting already planned).

Follow-up actions (assigned)

Taxonomy + decision tree
- [Rafael] Update Confluence draft taxonomy with workshop decisions + subtype list (due 2025-12-06)
- [Mei] Add 6–8 real incident examples mapped to categories (incl. capacity vs routing, streaming, private DNS/TLS) (due 2025-12-09)
- [Kira] Update decision tree draft with the new rules + “telemetry as primary vs contributing factor” guidance (due 2025-12-10)

Ownership mapping
- [Mina + Vanessa] Resolve the disputed cases list (capacity vs routing) and propose one primary owner per subtype (due 2025-12-11)
- [Ethan] Confirm control plane subtypes mapping (config write failures vs propagation) + escalation channel naming (due 2025-12-11)
- [Victor] Propose owner split for Private subtypes + which runbooks exist vs need writing (due 2025-12-12)

Tooling integration
- [Dylan + Devon] Incident-bot: required incident_type + subtype dropdown + “unknown” flow (ENG-4822) and closure gating for Sev0/Sev1 (target start 2025-12-09)
- [Allison] Provide metric label allowlist + sampling guidance for incident_type emission (ENG-4826 input) (due 2025-12-13)
- [Sergio] Draft first dashboard mock panels using the new taxonomy buckets (ENG-4828) (due 2025-12-16)
- [Mina] Routing: structured fallback_reason mapping notes to taxonomy (ENG-4824 input) (due 2025-12-13)

SLA alignment / comms
- [Aisha] Draft remediation SLA matrix v0 and identify where Dedicated/Private differ (sync with PM ticket PM-912) (due 2025-12-15)
- [Dev Patel] Draft Support/CS guidance snippet: how to communicate incident “type” without over-claiming root cause (due 2025-12-16)
- [Fiona] Security alignment: triggers for dual-tracking + audit expectations for auth-related incidents (due 2025-12-17)

Next milestones
- 12/06: taxonomy definitions updated in Confluence draft
- 12/10: decision tree updated + examples
- 12/12: ownership mapping draft circulated for review
- Week of 12/15: signoff round + begin incident-bot required fields rollout (pilot)

Open concerns captured (verbatim-ish)
- “If we make subtypes too granular, we’ll never get clean dashboards.” (Observability)
- “Primary owner needs to match who can actually mitigate at 2am, not who built it.” (SRE)
- “Support needs a consistent statement: what category it is, what mitigations are happening, what’s next update time.” (CS)

Appendix: disputed examples we walked through
1) Regional GPU event: elevated 5xx + increased fallback
- Data: fallback_reason=capacity, GPU utilization pegged, provisioning queue growing
- Decision: classify as Capacity/Fleet shortage (primary), contributing: Routing (if fallback behavior increases latency)

2) Latency regression after model rollout
- Data: p99 +40%, no error increase, correlates with model version X.Y
- Decision: Latency regression → subtype rollout/config regression; contributing: Runtime

3) Streaming resets spike after SDK upgrade (customer)
- Data: disconnect rate up, only a subset of clients, server metrics stable
- Decision: Streaming disconnects (primary), contributing: client SDK compatibility; mark customer-caused if validated

4) Control plane config write failures
- Data: customers cannot update routing policies; data plane serving still working
- Decision: Control plane outage/degradation (primary)

source.title: GA rollout checklist — Hosted overload protection (circuit breakers + load shedding)
source.body:
Doc purpose
This is the canary → GA rollout checklist for Hosted overload protection (circuit breakers + load shedding). It is written for the release lead + SRE oncall to run as a single source of truth for:
- Preconditions (what must be done before enabling in any prod region)
- Monitoring gates (what metrics must look like before we expand)
- Rollback triggers (hard stops and how to revert safely)
- Support readiness (docs, macros, escalation paths)
- Launch sign-offs (explicit owners)

Scope
Applies to the hosted API request path (gateway admission control + routing fallback + runtime backpressure integration) and customer-visible overload errors (ADR-014). Dedicated capacity and Private deployments are out of scope for GA sign-off here, except where shared code paths exist.

Definitions (operational)
- “Overload protection enabled”: gateway admission middleware evaluates policy + may shed; routing may attempt configured fallback; runtime exports saturation signals used by policy.
- “Shed”: request rejected early with stable overload taxonomy (e.g., 429/503) + correlation ID + Retry-After (where applicable).
- “Breaker open”: policy layer is actively preventing traffic to a route/model/region due to saturation/dependency health.

Owners (for paging / decisions)
- SRE lead / release IC: Rafael Mendes
- Dashboards/alerts: Aisha Bello
- Overload protection engineer (implementation triage): Christine Wong
- Gateway/control plane: Ethan Park (backup: Amara Diallo)
- Smart routing/fallback: Mina Haddad (backup: Ravi Menon)
- Runtime scheduling signals: Sofia Ivanova (backup: Omar El-Sayed)
- Product (customer semantics): Nikhil Sharma
- Support readiness: Dev Patel (escalations: Owen Phillips)
- Release/canary coordination: Connor O'Brien

Rollout plan (high-level)
Phase A — Regional canary (single region, small tenant slice)
Phase B — Expand to 2–3 regions + broader tenant slice
Phase C — GA (all hosted regions, tier-aware policy defaults)

CHECKLIST A: Preconditions (must be true before any prod canary)

A0. Change management / comms
[ ] Release window agreed (weekday, staffed hours for Eng + Support). Proposed window: Tue–Thu, 10:00–16:00 PT.
[ ] #eng-oncall and #support notified of canary window + expected new error codes.
[ ] Status page policy confirmed: we do NOT post for routine shedding under extreme load unless it causes customer-impacting sustained elevated error rates beyond SLO policy.

A1. Functional + compatibility requirements
[ ] Error payloads match ADR-014 in gateway AND downstream services. Verify:
    - stable error.code
    - message is customer-safe
    - correlation_id present
    - request_id present (if available)
    - retryable boolean (or equivalent contract)
[ ] HTTP status mapping validated for primary shed cases (expected defaults):
    - 429: tenant/route limit exceeded, token bucket / concurrency cap
    - 503: platform overloaded / breaker open / dependency brownout (when we want explicit “try later”)
[ ] Retry-After behavior consistent for shedding (especially 503). Confirm no missing header on expected routes (track SUP-2860 regression class).
[ ] Client retry contract validated internally: exponential backoff + jitter; respect Retry-After; no immediate tight-loop retries.

A2. Policy/config safety
[ ] Policy schema validation in place with safe defaults; no “footguns” allowing per-tenant config to disable global safety controls.
[ ] Tier defaults set (self-serve vs enterprise hosted):
    - self-serve more aggressive shedding (protect fleet)
    - enterprise hosted prefers fallback/queued admission (when configured) before shedding
[ ] Per-route/model selectors reviewed for cardinality risk (avoid per-tenant-per-model explosion in metrics).
[ ] Breaker open/close hysteresis configured to avoid flapping.

A3. Observability readiness (dashboards + alerts)
[ ] Dashboards deployed (observability-pack/pr-913) and verified accessible to SRE oncall.
[ ] Key panels present and populated in canary region:
    - shed_rate (by route/model-tier)
    - breaker_open_rate (by breaker type)
    - p95/p99 latency (overall + by model)
    - request timeout rate
    - 5xx rate (excluding overload-classified errors where applicable)
    - runtime saturation signals: queue depth, token/sec saturation, memory pressure proxy
    - fallback_attempt_rate + fallback_success_rate
[ ] Alert rules enabled in “ticket-only” mode for first 24h of canary (no paging) unless otherwise noted.
[ ] Runbook links embedded in alerts: hosted-overload-circuit-breaker-runbook + runtime overload mitigation.

A4. Load/perf validation
[ ] Perf-canary scenario executed with burst traffic profile; results attached/linked.
[ ] Evidence: under burst load, we shed predictably (429/503) and avoid timeouts/ambiguous 5xx.
[ ] Evidence: no retry storms observed in synthetic clients.
[ ] Evidence: fallback loop prevention works (bounded retries, no ping-pong between regions/models).

A5. Access + support ops
[ ] Support dashboard access confirmed (INT-771 resolved or temporary access granted).
[ ] Support macro drafted and reviewed: “What does overload error mean?” + “How to retry?” + correlation ID collection.
[ ] Known issues page updated (Support-facing) and includes escalation thresholds.
[ ] Internal escalation path documented (who to page by symptom).

A6. Rollback mechanism validated
[ ] Feature flags documented:
    - gateway admission shed enable/disable
    - routing fallback enable/disable
    - degradation modes (reduced max_tokens, streaming concurrency cap, tool-calling restrictions)
[ ] Rollback tested in staging: disabling flags returns platform to baseline behavior without deploy.

GO/NO-GO for canary start
[ ] Preconditions A0–A6 complete
[ ] SRE lead approves start

CHECKLIST B: Canary execution (Phase A)

B1. Canary scope
[ ] Region: __________ (recommended: us-east for traffic representativeness; avoid if already in incident state)
[ ] Tenant slice definition:
    - % of self-serve traffic: ____%
    - enterprise hosted tenants included: [ ] yes [ ] no (list if yes)
[ ] Models/routes in scope (start with a limited set): __________

B2. Enablement steps
[ ] Enable in “observe-only” mode where supported (emit breaker decisions but do not shed) for 30–60 min.
[ ] Verify:
    - decision_rate is non-zero under load
    - no unexpected “open” decisions at normal load
[ ] Enable active shedding for canary slice.
[ ] Confirm incident-bot announces breaker state transitions in #incidents/#eng-oncall.

B3. Monitoring gates (must hold for >= 2 hours during normal + at least one burst period)
PASS/FAIL criteria (fill in for canary region):
[ ] p95 latency increase vs baseline <= +5% (same time-of-day comparison)
[ ] timeout rate does not increase (target: flat or down)
[ ] 5xx rate (non-overload) does not increase (target: flat)
[ ] overload-classified responses are present when load is high (expected), but:
    - shed_rate sustained > 3% for > 15 min is investigated
    - breaker open sustained > 10 min is investigated
[ ] fallback success rate >= 70% when fallback is enabled for those routes (if fallback configured)
[ ] customer tickets: no pattern indicating missing Retry-After, confusing payloads, or SDK parsing failures

B4. Canary stop conditions (immediate pause and investigate)
[ ] Any evidence of retry storm: request rate increases after shedding, client behavior unstable
[ ] Missing Retry-After where expected (repeatable) OR correlation_id absent
[ ] Breaker flapping (open/close oscillation) causing elevated tail latency
[ ] Shed triggers at low load (false positives) with no corresponding saturation signals
[ ] Fallback loop or excessive fallback attempts per request
[ ] Measurable regression in successful request rate without a compensating reduction in timeouts

B5. Canary outcome decision
[ ] PASS: proceed to Phase B expansion
[ ] CONDITIONAL: tune thresholds / adjust hysteresis / update docs; re-run canary
[ ] FAIL: rollback (Checklist D) + open follow-up issues

CHECKLIST C: Progressive expansion + GA readiness (Phase B → C)

C1. Expansion plan
[ ] Add region(s): __________
[ ] Increase tenant slice to ____% self-serve
[ ] Enable enterprise hosted tenants only after support readiness confirmed + routing owners on-call coverage

C2. Monitoring gates for expansion (hold for 24 hours)
[ ] Global p95/p99 stable or improved under bursts
[ ] Error budget burn attributable to overload is tracked correctly per SLO policy
[ ] No sustained increase in customer-reported “mysterious timeouts” (goal: shift to explicit overload errors)
[ ] Support ticket volume manageable; macros resolving questions without engineering escalation for routine cases

C3. GA readiness checks (must be true before enabling globally)
[ ] Updated docs site content ready (developer-facing overload errors + retry guidance)
[ ] SDKs: at least one of Python/TypeScript has published docs/snippets for overload errors, OR support has ready workaround guidance
[ ] Oncall runbook finalized and reviewed (gateway + routing + runtime)
[ ] Alert paging policy set:
    - Page SRE if shed_rate > X% for Y min in >= 2 regions OR breaker open sustained Z min without recovery
    - Ticket-only for single-region short-lived shed spikes
[ ] Rollback triggers + procedures reviewed in live drill / game day OR tabletop exercise

CHECKLIST D: Rollback triggers + procedure (used at any phase)

D1. Hard rollback triggers (execute rollback immediately)
[ ] Overload protection causes a net increase in timeouts vs baseline for > 10 min
[ ] Overload protection causes widespread 5xx (non-overload) due to internal failures
[ ] Missing Retry-After/correlation_id regression affecting multiple tenants (repeatable)
[ ] Breaker system instability (flapping) causing elevated tail latency across regions
[ ] Routing fallback loop or cross-region thrash detected

D2. Rollback procedure (order matters)
1) [ ] Disable routing fallback first (stops loop/thrash risk)
2) [ ] Disable “degradation modes” (restore normal feature behavior)
3) [ ] Disable gateway shedding (or set to observe-only)
4) [ ] Confirm breaker decisions no longer enforced (metrics reflect)
5) [ ] Announce rollback in #eng-oncall with reason + dashboard link + next update time
6) [ ] If customer impact: coordinate with Product + Support on comms (template below)

D3. Post-rollback verification
[ ] Latency, timeouts, and 5xx return to baseline within 15–30 min
[ ] No residual elevated breaker-open metrics due to stale state
[ ] Open incidents/tickets updated with root cause hypothesis and next steps

Support/customer comms (templates)
- Internal (support):
  “We rolled back overload protection in <region> due to <symptom>. Customers may see timeouts rather than explicit overload errors temporarily. Please collect correlation_id + request_id + timestamp + region + model if customers report issues.”
- Customer-facing (if needed, approved by Product):
  “We identified an issue with our overload protection controls in <region> and have rolled back to restore normal service behavior. We will provide an update after validation.”

Sign-offs (required for GA enablement)
Fill in name + date + notes.
[ ] SRE (go/no-go): Rafael Mendes — ________
[ ] Dashboards/alerting readiness: Aisha Bello — ________
[ ] Gateway/control plane readiness: Ethan Park — ________
[ ] Routing/fallback readiness: Mina Haddad — ________
[ ] Runtime readiness: Sofia Ivanova — ________
[ ] Product/customer messaging: Nikhil Sharma — ________
[ ] Support readiness: Dev Patel — ________
[ ] Release coordination: Connor O'Brien — ________

Appendix: Metrics quick reference (what we look at first)
- “Are we overloaded or are we broken?”
  - If shed_rate ↑ and queue depth/saturation ↑: likely true overload (tune thresholds only if too aggressive)
  - If shed_rate ↑ but saturation flat: false positive (policy/signal bug)
- “Are we improving the customer experience under burst?”
  - Desired: fewer timeouts, fewer ambiguous 5xx, more explicit 429/503 with correlation IDs
- “Is fallback helping or hurting?”
  - Desired: fallback_success_rate high, bounded fallback attempts, no region ping-pong

Open questions / TODOs (as of last edit)
- Decide default Retry-After values per status (429 vs 503) and whether to vary by breaker reason.
- Confirm whether enterprise hosted tier should prefer queued admission (if available) vs immediate shedding (requires product/SLA alignment).
- Validate SLO accounting for overload-classified errors is consistent across dashboards, monthly reporting, and incident postmortems.

Doc purpose
This is the canary → GA rollout checklist for Hosted overload protection (circuit breakers + load shedding). It is written for the release lead + SRE oncall to run as a single source of truth for:
- Preconditions (what must be done before enabling in any prod region)
- Monitoring gates (what metrics must look like before we expand)
- Rollback triggers (hard stops and how to revert safely)
- Support readiness (docs, macros, escalation paths)
- Launch sign-offs (explicit owners)

Scope
Applies to the hosted API request path (gateway admission control + routing fallback + runtime backpressure integration) and customer-visible overload errors (ADR-014). Dedicated capacity and Private deployments are out of scope for GA sign-off here, except where shared code paths exist.

Definitions (operational)
- “Overload protection enabled”: gateway admission middleware evaluates policy + may shed; routing may attempt configured fallback; runtime exports saturation signals used by policy.
- “Shed”: request rejected early with stable overload taxonomy (e.g., 429/503) + correlation ID + Retry-After (where applicable).
- “Breaker open”: policy layer is actively preventing traffic to a route/model/region due to saturation/dependency health.

Owners (for paging / decisions)
- SRE lead / release IC: Rafael Mendes
- Dashboards/alerts: Aisha Bello
- Overload protection engineer (implementation triage): Christine Wong
- Gateway/control plane: Ethan Park (backup: Amara Diallo)

[ ] Client retry contract validated internally: exponential backoff + jitter; respect Retry-After; no immediate tight-loop retries.

A2. Policy/config safety

A4. Load/perf validation

[ ] Evidence: no retry storms observed in synthetic clients.

A6. Rollback mechanism validated

GO/NO-GO for canary start

CHECKLIST B: Canary execution (Phase A)

B1. Canary scope

[ ] Updated docs site content ready (developer-facing overload errors + retry guidance)
[ ] SDKs: at least one of Python/TypeScript has published docs/snippets for overload errors, OR support has ready workaround guidance
[ ] Oncall runbook finalized and reviewed (gateway + routing + runtime)
[ ] Alert paging policy set:
    - Page SRE if shed_rate > X% for Y min in >= 2 regions OR breaker open sustained Z min without recovery
    - Ticket-only for single-region short-lived shed spikes
[ ] Rollback triggers + procedures reviewed in live drill / game day OR tabletop exercise

C2. Monitoring gates for expansion (hold for 24 hours)
[ ] Global p95/p99 stable or improved under bursts
[ ] Error budget burn attributable to overload is tracked correctly per SLO policy
[ ] No sustained increase in customer-reported “mysterious timeouts” (goal: shift to explicit overload errors)
[ ] Support ticket volume manageable; macros resolving questions without engineering escalation for routine cases

C3. GA readiness checks (must be true before enabling globally)
[ ] Updated docs site content ready (developer-facing overload errors + retry guidance)
[ ] SDKs: at least one of Python/TypeScript has published docs/snippets for overload errors, OR support has ready workaround guidance
[ ] Oncall runbook finalized and reviewed (gateway + routing + runtime)
[ ] Alert paging policy set:
    - Page SRE if shed_rate > X% for Y min in >= 2 regions OR breaker open sustained Z min without recovery
    - Ticket-only for single-region short-lived shed spikes
[ ] Rollback triggers + procedures reviewed in live drill / game day OR tabletop exercise

CHECKLIST D: Rollback triggers + procedure (used at any phase)

D1. Hard rollback triggers (execute rollback immediately)
[ ] Overload protection causes a net increase in timeouts vs baseline for > 10 min
[ ] Overload protection causes widespread 5xx (non-overload) due to internal failures
[ ] Missing Retry-After/correlation_id regression affecting multiple tenants (repeatable)
[ ] Breaker system instability (flapping) causing elevated tail latency across regions
[ ] Routing fallback loop or cross-region thrash detected

D2. Rollback procedure (order matters)
1) [ ] Disable routing fallback first (stops loop/thrash risk)
2) [ ] Disable “degradation modes” (restore normal feature behavior)
3) [ ] Disable gateway shedding (or set to observe-only)
4) [ ] Confirm breaker decisions no longer enforced (metrics reflect)
5) [ ] Announce rollback in #eng-oncall with reason + dashboard link + next update time
6) [ ] If customer impact: coordinate with Product + Support on comms (template below)

D3. Post-rollback verification
[ ] Latency, timeouts, and 5xx return to baseline within 15–30 min
[ ] No residual elevated breaker-open metrics due to stale state
[ ] Open incidents/tickets updated with root cause hypothesis and next steps

Support/customer comms (templates)
- Internal (support):
  “We rolled back overload protection in <region> due to <symptom>. Customers may see timeouts rather than explicit overload errors temporarily. Please collect correlation_id + request_id + timestamp + region + model if customers report issues.”
- Customer-facing (if needed, approved by Product):
  “We identified an issue with our overload protection controls in <region> and have rolled back to restore normal service behavior. We will provide an update after validation.”

Sign-offs (required for GA enablement)
Fill in name + date + notes.
[ ] SRE (go/no-go): Rafael Mendes — ________
[ ] Dashboards/alerting readiness: Aisha Bello — ________
[ ] Gateway/control plane readiness: Ethan Park — ________
[ ] Routing/fallback readiness: Mina Haddad — ________
[ ] Runtime readiness: Sofia Ivanova — ________
[ ] Product/customer messaging: Nikhil Sharma — ________
[ ] Support readiness: Dev Patel — ________
[ ] Release coordination: Connor O'Brien — ________

Appendix: Metrics quick reference (what we look at first)
- “Are we overloaded or are we broken?”
  - If shed_rate ↑ and queue depth/saturation ↑: likely true overload (tune thresholds only if too aggressive)
  - If shed_rate ↑ but saturation flat: false positive (policy/signal bug)
- “Are we improving the customer experience under burst?”
  - Desired: fewer timeouts, fewer ambiguous 5xx, more explicit 429/503 with correlation IDs
- “Is fallback helping or hurting?”
  - Desired: fallback_success_rate high, bounded fallback attempts, no region ping-pong

Open questions / TODOs (as of last edit)
- Decide default Retry-After values per status (429 vs 503) and whether to vary by breaker reason.
- Confirm whether enterprise hosted tier should prefer queued admission (if available) vs immediate shedding (requires product/SLA alignment).
- Validate SLO accounting for overload-classified errors is consistent across dashboards, monthly reporting, and incident postmortems.

source.title: Canary plan (draft) — Mixed-workload scheduler (short/long queues)
source.content:
NOTE: This is a pre-Confluence draft intended to align Runtime + SRE + Releases on a safe, limited production canary for the mixed-workload scheduler prototype (short/long prompt-aware policy). Expect edits after SRE review + final benchmark readout.

1) Objective
- Primary objective: reduce user-visible tail latency (p95) for mixed prompt-length distributions (short chat prompts interleaved with long-context prompts) without materially regressing throughput or causing fairness/starvation issues.
- Secondary objective: validate that instrumentation (per-class metrics + traces) is sufficient to catch failure modes early (starvation, misclassification, kernel thrash, cache eviction spikes).

2) What is being enabled
- Feature flag: scheduler.short_long_class_policy (name TBD in config system; Benji to confirm final key).
- Policy: two-queue dispatch (short vs long) with aging/starvation prevention + per-iteration token budgeting guardrails.
- Classification inputs: prompt/context length and/or predicted prefill cost (depending on final wiring in runtime-1.18).
- Scope: only selected Hosted API pools + one Dedicated pool (if we have an opted-in account / internal test customer). No global default changes.

Non-goals for canary
- No customer-configurable classes/tiers. We only validate internal knobs and safe bounds.
- No changes to admission control besides existing backpressure behavior.

3) Preconditions / gating checks (must be true before step 0)
- Code shipped in runtime-1.18 canary candidate with:
  - Two-queue policy + aging fix (includes regression test for starvation edge case).
  - Per-class metrics emitted (queue depth, time in queue, starvation counters, dispatch share, per-class p50/p95 where applicable).
  - Config knobs plumbed with validation and safe bounds (min/max values).
- Perf-canary nightly results show no regressions vs baseline on representative mixes (80/20, 50/50, long-spike) for at least 2 consecutive nights.
- Dashboards ready and reviewed with SRE (Allison/Aisha) + alert routing to #eng-runtime and #eng-sre.
- Rollback mechanism validated (flag flip + config revert) with documented ETA and owner.

4) Canary targets (initial)
Hosted API (recommended starting point)
- Region: us-east (lowest blast radius + best on-call coverage) or the smallest us-east pool segment.
- Pool selection: pick 1–2 low-to-mid volume pools that still see mixed traffic (Julian/Logan to confirm actual mix via traces). Avoid the largest pool for first 24h.
- Traffic segmentation: if possible, route a consistent shard (e.g., % of requests by hash) rather than “random per-request” to simplify before/after comparisons.

Dedicated (optional, only if we have explicit opt-in)
- Candidate: one Dedicated pool with known mixed-workload pain. If customer opt-in is not finalized, use internal Dedicated test pool only.
- Account note: if a real customer is used, confirm written opt-in and comms plan (CS/Sales to coordinate; reference SUP tickets only internally).

5) Ramp schedule (proposed)
Assumes runtime-1.18 canary release is already in place and we’re only toggling scheduler policy.

Day 0 (Preparation window)
- Validate dashboards/alerts in prod (metrics present even when flag off).
- Confirm current baseline p95/TTFT and throughput for target pools over last 24h; capture “before” snapshot links.
- Ensure rollback contacts and escalation chain are staffed.

Step 1: 1% traffic (or smallest shard) — 2 hours
- Enable feature flag for 1% of traffic in target Hosted API pool(s).
- Keep conservative parameter set (see Section 6).
- Monitor continuously for the first 30 minutes.

Step 2: 5% — 4 hours
- If Step 1 passes, ramp to 5%.
- Re-check per-class metrics for starvation signals and queue skew.

Step 3: 20% — 12–24 hours (overnight ok if staffed)
- Ramp to 20% for at least one diurnal cycle if possible.
- Goal: validate performance under naturally occurring long-context spikes.

Step 4: 50% — 24–48 hours
- Ramp to 50% after SRE sign-off based on Step 3 results.
- If stable, hold for 1–2 days and compare against control shard.

Step 5: 100% of target pool(s) — 24 hours
- Only after meeting success criteria and no rollback triggers.
- Still limited to selected pools/region. This is not “global enable.”

Dedicated optional ramp (if enabled)
- Start at 5% equivalent or smallest feasible shard in the selected Dedicated pool for 24 hours.
- Do not ramp Dedicated and Hosted simultaneously unless on-call staffing is doubled.

6) Initial parameter set (conservative defaults for canary)
Exact knob names depend on final config plumbing; below is conceptual.
- short_long_threshold_tokens: start with trace-informed threshold (from production distribution summary). If uncertain, choose a threshold that classifies ~70–85% of requests as “short” in the target pool.
- short_queue_burst_cap: enabled (prevents starving long under sustained short load).
- aging_half_life / aging_rate: enabled with “known-safe” setting from load tests (post edge-case fix).
- per_iteration_token_budget:
  - enforce a max share per class per scheduling iteration; ensure long queue always receives minimum budget over time.
- max_queue_skew: set to prevent pathological backlog accumulation.

Parameter changes during canary
- Treat parameter tweaks as changes requiring a mini-approval (Runtime TL + SRE on-call). Log all changes in the rollout thread with timestamps and rationale.

7) Success metrics (pass/fail)
Primary success criteria (Hosted API)
- Tail latency: p95 end-to-end latency improves for mixed workload routes/models by >= 10% relative to control shard OR absolute improvement meets PM target for the top affected route(s).
- TTFT (time-to-first-token): no sustained regression > 5% at p95 for short-class traffic.

Guardrail metrics (must not regress)
- Throughput (tokens/sec per GPU or equivalent pool throughput): no sustained regression > 3–5% vs control over the measurement window.
- Error rates: no increase in 5xx, timeouts, or client cancellations beyond normal variance (SRE to define exact thresholds per pool).
- Fairness/progress:
  - Long-class starvation counters remain at 0 (or below agreed tolerance) and time-in-queue for long does not show unbounded growth.
  - Dispatch share for long remains above minimum floor (config-dependent).
- Stability:
  - No increase in GPU instability symptoms: kernel selection thrash signals, excessive cache eviction spikes, or “oscillating batch shape” patterns (per profiling guidance).

Dedicated success criteria (if used)
- p95 latency reduction on the customer’s reported workload mix, with no SLO violations and no starvation signals.

8) Monitoring checklist (during ramp)
Dashboards
- Scheduler class metrics dashboard (per-class queue depth, time-in-queue, starvation counters, dispatch share).
- Route/model latency (p50/p95/p99) split by shard (canary vs control) where available.
- TTFT dashboard for chat routes (esp. short-class).
- GPU utilization + tokens/sec + batch size distribution.
- Error/timeout/cancellation rate.

What to look for (early warning)
- Long queue depth increasing monotonically while short queue remains stable.
- Starvation counter increments or long time-in-queue p95 trending upward without recovery.
- Sudden oscillations in batch composition leading to latency spikes (possible kernel selection instability).
- Increased cache eviction / KV cache compaction spikes correlated with mixed traffic.

9) Rollback criteria (hard triggers)
Immediate rollback (flag off + revert params) if any occur for > 10 minutes (unless SRE explicitly overrides due to known external cause):
- p95 end-to-end latency regression >= 15% vs control shard for key Hosted API routes/models in the target pool.
- TTFT p95 regression >= 10% for short-class traffic.
- Any sustained starvation signal:
  - starvation counter increments above 0 in production on the target pool, OR
  - long time-in-queue p95 exceeds baseline by >= 25% and is trending upward.
- Error budget risk:
  - 5xx/timeout rate crosses SRE-defined threshold or triggers paging.
- Incident-level symptoms (GPU resets, widespread request failures) correlated with enablement.

Soft rollback / hold (pause ramp, investigate) if:
- Throughput regression 3–5% sustained for > 30 minutes.
- Mixed results: p95 improves but p99 worsens; require review by Runtime TL + SRE before proceeding.

Rollback procedure (summary)
- Flip feature flag off for the canary shard(s).
- Revert any tuned parameters to baseline defaults.
- Confirm recovery on primary dashboards for at least 30 minutes.
- Post incident/rollback note in #eng-releases thread with timestamps, what changed, and next steps.

10) Ownership / staffing
Roles
- Rollout driver: Rafael Mendes
- Runtime decision maker (policy/params): Lena Fischer
- Runtime on-call escalation: Sofia Ivanova
- SRE on-call sponsor: Sean Gallagher
- SLO/alert monitoring: Aisha Bello
- Dashboards/metrics: Allison Grant (with Julian Moore for runtime metric correctness)
- Config/flag plumbing: Benji Okafor
- Release coordination: Connor O’Brien

Coverage plan (proposed)
- Step 1/2 ramps must occur during business hours PT/ET overlap.
- Step 3 overnight hold is allowed only if:
  - SRE paging thresholds are set, and
  - a named driver is available for the first 2 hours after ramp.

Comms
- Single rollout thread in #eng-releases with checklist and links.
- Cross-post key updates to #eng-runtime and #eng-sre.

11) Open questions / TODOs
- Confirm final config keys + safe bounds (Benji).
- Confirm exact target pools and shard mechanism (Rafael + Logan).
- Decide whether Dedicated is in-scope for this first canary (Lauren + Sean + Sofia).
- Finalize pass/fail thresholds with PM stakeholders (Nikhil/Lauren) and SRE error budget policy.
- Attach dashboard links + query IDs once created.

12) Appendix: quick links to collect before canary
- Perf-canary run(s): mixed workload job results (nightly).
- Benchmark-lab comparison report for runtime-1.18 candidate vs baseline.
- Baseline production snapshot: p95/TTFT/throughput for target pools (24h).
- Slack threads: rollout coordination + any known issues/edge case notes.

Change log
- 2025-11-26: initial draft created.
- 2025-12-02: updated rollback criteria and ramp schedule based on starvation edge-case fix and SRE feedback in #eng-sre thread.
